proposals.title: Wide-Temperature-Range Thermal Conductivity of Quantum Composite Materials with Charge-Density-Wave Fillers
proposals.motivation: Understanding thermal transport across a broad temperature range is critical for the design of next-generation electronic and quantum devices, where heat dissipation limits performance and reliability. Pre-2026 work on quantum composites with charge-density-wave (CDW) fillers has shown promise for tunable electronic properties, but their thermal conductivity, especially its temperature dependence from cryogenic to high temperatures, remains unexplored, creating a knowledge gap for thermal management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thermal conductivity of quantum composites with CDW fillers will exhibit non-monotonic and anomalous temperature dependence due to the interplay of CDW phase transitions, quantum confinement effects, and varying phonon scattering regimes across the temperature spectrum.
proposals.method: We will fabricate quantum composite thin films, integrating CDW materials (e.g., 1T-TaS2) into a boron nitride or polymer matrix, building on Balandin's expertise in boron nitride phononics and composite synthesis. Thermal conductivity (κ) will be measured from ~10 K to 600 K using a combination of the 3ω method for the lower range and time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR) for higher temperatures. Electronic and structural characterization (Raman spectroscopy, low-frequency noise measurements) will be performed in parallel to correlate thermal transport with charge density wave phase stability and electronic noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive thermal transport map for quantum CDW composites, directly linking thermal conductivity anomalies to electronic phase transitions. It will provide essential data for modeling heat flow in emerging quantum materials and inform the design of composites with tailored thermal and electronic properties for extreme-environment applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically probing the thermal conductivity of quantum composites across the full temperature range where CDW phases are active, an approach not yet reported pre-2026, and in correlating thermal transport directly with electronic phase transitions and low-frequency noise characteristics.

proposals.title: Non-contact optothermal Raman mapping of thermal conductivity in two-dimensional boron nitride polymorphs
proposals.motivation: Understanding thermal transport in 2D boron nitride (BN) is critical for its integration into next-generation electronic and optoelectronic devices, where heat dissipation limits performance and reliability. While the optothermal Raman technique has been successfully applied to graphene, its transfer to BN—a wide-bandgap insulator with different phonon physics—remains unexplored, creating a significant knowledge gap in the thermal management of ultra-wide-bandgap electronics.
proposals.hypothesis: The non-contact micro-Raman optothermal method, previously established for graphene, can be quantitatively adapted to measure the anisotropic in-plane thermal conductivity of various 2D BN polymorphs (e.g., hexagonal and turbostratic BN), revealing how structural disorder and stacking order govern heat conduction in these electrically insulating van der Waals materials.
proposals.method: We will employ a confocal micro-Raman system with dual-wavelength lasers: a focused pump laser (e.g., 488 nm) to locally heat suspended and substrate-supported BN flakes, and a low-power probe laser to measure the Stokes Raman peak shift of the E2g phonon mode, which is sensitive to temperature. The thermal conductivity will be extracted by fitting the spatial profile of the temperature rise, obtained from the calibrated Raman peak shift, to the solution of the steady-state heat diffusion equation. This will be systematically applied to mechanically exfoliated and CVD-grown BN films of varying thickness and stacking order, with cross-validation using electrical thermometry on fabricated nanodevices where applicable.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first non-contact thermal conductivity database for 2D BN films, directly linking vibrational spectra (from Balandin's prior work on BN phonons) to thermal transport. It will provide essential design rules for using BN as a thermal management layer in high-power electronics and quantum composites, and validate a universal optothermal metrology for insulating 2D materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting a laser-heating, Raman-based thermometry technique—previously confined to conductive materials like graphene—to accurately probe thermal transport in insulating 2D BN, and in correlating the measured thermal conductivity directly with specific structural polymorphs and stacking disorders identified via Raman spectroscopy.

proposals.title: Engineering Phonon Transport via Edge-Functionalized Graphene for Ultralow Thermal Boundary Resistance
proposals.motivation: Graphene exhibits intrinsically low thermal boundary (Kapitza) resistance, making it a prime candidate for thermal management in next-generation electronics and quantum devices. However, the assumption of pristine interfaces limits further optimization. Pre-2026 work on quantum composites and phonon spectra in layered materials suggests that targeted interface modifications could actively enhance phonon coupling. This research aims to move beyond the intrinsic limit by designing and controlling graphene interfaces to achieve unprecedented thermal conductance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intentional covalent functionalization of graphene edges, as opposed to basal planes, will selectively modify the phonon density of states and coupling coefficients, thereby reducing the thermal boundary resistance below the values measured for pristine graphene interfaces.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize graphene flakes with controlled edge functionalization using hydrogen, fluorine, and hydroxyl groups. Thermal boundary resistance will be measured using a micro-fabricated optothermal Raman technique, correlating temperature rise with laser power on suspended membranes. Low-frequency noise spectroscopy, a core expertise of the PI, will be employed as a sensitive probe of charge-phonon interactions and defect dynamics at the functionalized interfaces. Electronic transport and temperature-dependent measurements will disentangle the contributions of acoustic and optical phonons to the heat transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials-by-design principle for thermal interface engineering, demonstrating that functionalization can be beneficial rather than detrimental to phonon transport. It will provide a quantitative framework linking specific chemical modifications at graphene edges to phonon transmission spectra, directly impacting the design of quantum composites and wide-bandgap semiconductor devices where heat dissipation is critical.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 studies focus on preserving graphene's pristine state or functionalizing basal planes for electronic properties, the novelty lies in intentionally using edge-selective chemistry as a targeted tool to improve, not degrade, thermal phonon coupling, challenging the conventional wisdom for thermal interfaces.

proposals.title: In-Plane Thermal Conductivity Enhancement in Quantum Composites via Aligned Charge-Density-Wave Fillers for Lateral Heat Spreading
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of modern high-power electronic and optoelectronic devices, such as Ga2O3 diodes, critically depends on efficient lateral heat spreading to prevent hot-spot formation. While prior work on thermal interface materials has focused on maximizing cross-plane conductivity, the in-plane thermal transport within composite layers remains a significant bottleneck. This proposal addresses the need for composite materials engineered specifically for superior in-plane thermal conductivity to enable effective lateral heat dissipation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that aligning high-thermal-conductivity, two-dimensional charge-density-wave (CDW) filler flakes (e.g., 1T-TaS2) within a polymer matrix will create percolating pathways that dramatically enhance the composite's in-plane thermal conductivity, surpassing the performance of composites optimized solely for cross-plane transport.
proposals.method: We will fabricate composites using solution-processable CDW fillers and employ shear-force alignment techniques to orient the flakes in-plane within an epoxy matrix. The thermal conductivity will be characterized using frequency-domain thermoreflectance (FDTR) for cross-plane measurements and the 3ω method for in-plane measurements. Electronic and low-frequency noise spectroscopy will be used to correlate thermal transport with the filler alignment quality and CDW phase stability under operational temperatures and currents.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials design framework for optimizing in-plane thermal transport in composites, directly addressing thermal bottlenecks in lateral device geometries. It will provide the first systematic study linking aligned CDW filler morphology, phase stability, and anisotropic thermal conductivity, offering a new strategy for thermal management in power electronics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the composite optimization paradigm from exclusive cross-plane focus to deliberate in-plane thermal pathway engineering, specifically utilizing the unique properties of aligned quantum CDW materials as fillers, which have not been explored for this purpose.

proposals.title: High-Temperature Thermal Conductivity of Quantum Composite Materials with Charge-Density-Wave Fillers via Extended Laser Flash Analysis
proposals.motivation: The development of wide-bandgap semiconductors like β-Ga2O3 for high-power electronics is limited by thermal management challenges at elevated operational temperatures. Pre-2026 work on quantum composites with charge-density-wave (CDW) fillers has shown promise for thermal conductivity enhancement at room temperature, but their performance and stability across the full 300–800 K range critical for device reliability remains uncharacterized. Extending accurate thermal transport measurements to these higher temperatures is essential for practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that quantum composites incorporating CDW fillers will maintain a significant, non-monotonic enhancement in thermal conductivity up to 800 K due to the interplay of filler phonon spectra and phase transitions, which can be precisely mapped using an extended laser flash technique.
proposals.method: We will employ a modified laser flash analysis (LFA) system, integrating a high-power, pulsed Nd:YAG laser (1064 nm) and an InSb infrared detector calibrated for high-temperature operation. Thin-film samples of β-(AlxGa1−x)2O3 and CDW-composite heterostructures will be prepared via pulsed laser deposition. The standard LFA protocol will be extended by implementing a three-layer model and a custom pulse-shaping algorithm to deconvolute radiative heat losses and substrate effects at temperatures exceeding 600 K. Temperature-dependent thermal diffusivity will be correlated with Raman spectroscopy measurements of optical and acoustic phonons.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-fidelity dataset on the high-temperature thermal conductivity of emerging quantum composite materials, directly informing their integration into high-power electronic and photonic devices. It will establish a robust experimental framework for characterizing thermal transport in extreme environments, bridging the gap between novel material synthesis and practical thermal management solutions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying and validating the laser flash technique for precise thermal characterization of quantum composite materials at temperatures where conventional methods fail due to dominant radiative transfer, specifically targeting the unexplored high-temperature behavior of CDW fillers known for their strong electron-phonon coupling and phase transitions.

proposals.title: DNA-Origami-Assembled Graphene Quantum Composites for Controlled Low-Frequency Noise and Charge-Density-Wave Transport
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in quantum composites, such as those with charge-density-wave fillers, highlights the critical role of filler arrangement in governing macroscopic electronic and noise properties. However, achieving deterministic, nanoscale spatial control of conductive flakes within a matrix remains a significant materials engineering challenge, limiting the optimization of quantum transport and noise performance in devices like Schottky diodes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using DNA origami as a programmable scaffold to position graphene flakes with atomic-layer precision will enable the creation of composite materials with tunable percolation pathways, leading to superior control over low-frequency electronic noise and emergent charge-density-wave phenomena.
proposals.method: We will synthesize rectangular DNA origami tiles (~100 nm x 70 nm) with specific ssDNA capture strands at designed positions. Chemically exfoliated graphene flakes will be functionalized with complementary ssDNA handles. The assembly will be driven by sequence-specific hybridization in solution, creating pre-defined 2D arrays of flakes on the DNA scaffold. These DNA-flake complexes will be integrated into an insulating polymer matrix (e.g., PMMA). The electronic characterization will involve fabricating two-terminal devices to measure current-voltage characteristics, low-frequency 1/f noise spectra as a function of charge density and temperature, and searching for signatures of collective CDW transport modulated by the engineered flake geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials platform for fabricating composites with designed nanoscale conductive networks, directly linking atomic-scale arrangement to macroscopic quantum transport. It will provide fundamental insights into how engineered disorder affects 1/f noise generation and the stability of correlated electron states in confined geometries, advancing the field of tailored electronic materials.
proposals.novelty: While DNA origami has been used to arrange nanoparticles and nanowires, its application to deterministically position atomically thin van der Waals flakes like graphene to construct functional quantum composites for noise and transport studies is a novel and unexplored synthesis paradigm.

proposals.title: Roll-to-Roll Manufacturing of Large-Area Quantum Composite Films for High-Performance Thermal Management
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of high-power electronics and optoelectronics is a critical bottleneck, demanding scalable production of advanced thermal interface materials (TIMs). Pre-2026 research, including on quantum composites with charge-density-wave fillers, has demonstrated the exceptional thermal properties of low-dimensional materials, but their fabrication remains limited to small-scale, lab-based processes. Adapting high-throughput, roll-to-roll (R2R) manufacturing, proven for flexible electronics, is essential to transition these novel materials from proof-of-concept to practical, large-area applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the shear-alignment and controlled deposition dynamics of a roll-to-roll coating process can be optimized to produce uniform, large-area films of quantum composite materials (e.g., incorporating graphene, boron nitride, or charge-density-wave fillers) with superior and consistent thermal conductivity, while preserving the filler's intrinsic phonon and electronic properties.
proposals.method: The method will adapt a pilot-scale R2R system with slot-die coating for precise deposition of composite inks onto flexible substrates (e.g., polyimide). The composite formulation will be based on Balandin's prior work, utilizing fillers like exfoliated graphene or hexagonal boron nitride in a polymer matrix. Process parameters—including web speed, shear rate, temperature, and post-deposition annealing—will be systematically varied. The resulting films will be characterized using Raman spectroscopy for filler alignment and strain, scanning electron microscopy for morphology, and the 3-omega technique for cross-plane thermal conductivity. Low-frequency noise spectroscopy will be employed to assess electronic uniformity and defect density in films intended for active device integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational manufacturing protocol for scalable production of high-performance TIMs, directly linking R2R process parameters to the thermal and electronic properties of quantum composite films. It will provide quantitative data on the trade-offs between throughput, film quality, and thermal performance, offering a crucial pathway for industrial adoption. The project will also extend the application of noise spectroscopy as a quality control tool for large-area manufactured electronic materials.
proposals.novelty: While R2R is established for flexible electronics, its application to fabricate thermal management materials based on quantum composites with carefully engineered phonon spectra is novel. The integration of acoustic and thermal property characterization with electronic noise diagnostics to optimize a manufacturing process for thermal materials represents a distinct, interdisciplinary approach beyond current state-of-the-art.

proposals.title: Low-Noise, High-Thermal-Conductivity Nanocomposite Fillers for Nano-Gap Thermal Bridging in Advanced Heterogeneous Integration
proposals.motivation: Advanced semiconductor packaging, such as 3D-ICs and heterogeneous integration, faces severe thermal bottlenecks due to nanometer-scale air gaps at material interfaces, which impede heat dissipation and degrade device reliability. Pre-2026 research has identified the need for novel thermal interface materials (TIMs) that can conform to these nano-gaps while maintaining high thermal conductivity and low electrical noise, the latter being critical for sensitive analog and quantum-inspired circuits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a nanocomposite incorporating charge-density-wave (CDW) fillers, such as 1T-TaS2, within a boron nitride (BN) matrix will provide superior thermal bridging of nano-gaps due to enhanced phonon transport across interfaces and will simultaneously exhibit suppressed low-frequency electronic noise compared to conventional metal- or graphene-based fillers.
proposals.method: We will synthesize quantum composites by embedding exfoliated CDW filler crystals (e.g., 1T-TaS2, TiSe2) into a turbostratic BN matrix using solution processing and controlled deposition. The thermal conductivity of thin-film composites will be measured via transient thermoreflectance, while their effectiveness in filling model nano-gaps (fabricated using e-beam lithography) will be assessed through device-level thermal resistance testing. Concurrently, low-frequency (1/f) noise spectroscopy will be performed on test Schottky diode structures incorporating the nanocomposite as an interfacial layer to evaluate its electronic stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of multifunctional TIMs that simultaneously address thermal management and electronic noise challenges in advanced packaging. It will provide fundamental insights into phonon and charge transport across confined interfaces in quantum composite systems, directly informing the design of next-generation integrated circuits.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate use of CDW materials—typically studied for their electronic phase transitions—as thermal fillers, leveraging their unique phonon spectra and suppressed noise characteristics, combined with a BN matrix known for its high thermal conductivity and electrical insulation, a combination not explored for nano-gap bridging.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Ultra-Low Cross-Plane Thermal Conductivity Composites via High-Conductivity Pathway Mapping and Quantum Composite Principles
proposals.motivation: Efficient thermal insulation is critical for thermoelectrics, electronics packaging, and aerospace applications. While significant research has focused on discovering high-thermal-conductivity materials, a systematic inverse design approach to minimize cross-plane conductivity in composites, by strategically disrupting phonon transport pathways, remains underdeveloped. This work addresses this gap by leveraging insights from the study of high-conductivity networks to create their thermal-insulating inverse.
proposals.hypothesis: By mapping and then deliberately disrupting the primary high-conductivity pathways within a composite matrix—through the strategic inclusion of fillers that introduce strong phonon scattering interfaces and localized resonant modes—we can achieve a greater suppression of cross-plane thermal transport than through random dispersion or conventional interface engineering alone.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental framework. First, finite-element and Boltzmann transport equation simulations will model phonon transport in candidate matrices (e.g., BN thin films, polymers) to identify dominant conductive networks. An inverse design algorithm will then optimize the placement and morphology of fillers, such as charge-density-wave materials (e.g., 1T-TaS2) or tailored nanoparticles, to maximally scatter acoustic and optical phonons along the cross-plane direction. These designed composites will be fabricated, and their cross-plane thermal conductivity will be measured using frequency-domain thermoreflectance, correlated with low-frequency noise spectroscopy to probe defect and interface-induced scattering.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel inverse-design methodology for thermal management materials, specifically targeting ultra-low cross-plane conductivity. It will yield new composite architectures with validated thermal insulation performance and provide fundamental insights into the relationship between engineered disorder in filler networks and the suppression of specific phonon modes.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying an inverse problem framework—typically used for high-conductivity design—explicitly to minimize thermal transport, and in integrating the unique phonon-scattering properties of quantum materials like charge-density-wave fillers, informed by noise spectroscopy, into the composite design loop.

proposals.title: Quantum-Confined Boron Nitride Composites for High Thermal Conductivity and Electrical Insulation in Power Electronics
proposals.motivation: The advancement of wide-bandgap power electronics, such as those based on Ga2O3 and GaN, is limited by thermal management challenges. Effective heat spreaders must possess high thermal conductivity while being electrically insulating to prevent leakage currents and noise, which degrade device performance and reliability. Current composite materials struggle to simultaneously optimize these dual properties, creating a critical bottleneck for next-generation high-power-density circuits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite matrix of turbostratic boron nitride (t-BN), infused with quantum-confined, electrically insulating fillers that possess intrinsic charge-density-wave (CDW) states, will decouple phonon and electron transport. This will yield a material with exceptionally high in-plane thermal conductivity and maximized electrical resistivity, surpassing the performance of conventional polymer-ceramic composites.
proposals.method: We will fabricate composites using a solution-processed matrix of exfoliated t-BN flakes, known for high in-plane thermal conductivity. As fillers, we will synthesize and integrate low-dimensional (1D/2D) materials with insulating CDW ground states (e.g., TaS3, (TaSe4)2I), which suppress free carrier density. The filler morphology, concentration, and interface will be engineered via sonication and hot-pressing. We will characterize thermal conductivity using the 3ω method and Raman thermometry, while electrical insulation will be assessed via high-voltage breakdown tests and low-frequency noise spectroscopy to probe trap states and leakage mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for thermal interface materials by leveraging quantum-confined CDW fillers to block electron transport without scattering phonons. It will provide a foundational material platform for thermally managing high-voltage, high-frequency electronics where electrical isolation is paramount. The study will also deliver crucial structure-property relationships linking filler quantum state, composite microstructure, and dual thermal/electrical performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate use of quantum materials with charge-density-wave states not for their electronic activity, but as electrically 'dead,' phonon-transparent fillers to enhance insulation in a thermally conductive BN matrix. This approach moves beyond simple dielectric ceramics by utilizing a filler's intrinsic quantum ground state to achieve the property decoupling.

proposals.title: Quantum Confinement and Low-Frequency 1/f Noise in Ultrathin Charge-Density-Wave Nanowires and Atomic Chains
proposals.motivation: The scaling of electronic devices to atomic-scale one-dimensional (1D) structures, such as nanowires and atomic chains, is a fundamental frontier in nanoelectronics. However, the electronic transport and noise properties in these extreme quantum-confined systems are poorly understood, especially for correlated electron materials. Pre-2026 work has established that 1/f noise is a sensitive probe of material quality and electronic correlations, but its behavior in 1D systems with charge-density-wave (CDW) order remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extreme quantum confinement in ultrathin CDW nanowires and atomic chains will fundamentally alter their phase transition dynamics and electronic scattering, leading to a characteristic, quantized evolution of low-frequency 1/f noise that can be modeled by extending the Hooge's relation with a confinement-modified effective carrier number.
proposals.method: We will synthesize ultrathin nanowires and atomic chains of model CDW materials (e.g., TaS3, NbSe3) using chemical vapor transport and precise exfoliation. Electronic transport and low-frequency noise spectroscopy will be performed in a variable-temperature cryostat (4.2-300 K) with ultra-low-noise preamplifiers. The experimental data will be analyzed using a modified Hooge model, fS_I/I^2 = α_H / (N_eff f^γ), where N_eff is the effective number of correlated carriers in the confined CDW state, and γ is the noise exponent. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations will support the interpretation of confinement effects on electronic structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental framework linking quantum confinement in correlated 1D materials to their low-frequency noise signatures. It will provide critical metrics for the stability and performance of future atomic-scale electronic and quantum devices incorporating CDW elements. The results will extend the applicability of noise spectroscopy to the ultimate limit of material scaling.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Balandin's group has studied noise in bulk CDW composites and wide-bandgap diodes, applying these precise noise metrology techniques to synthetically confined, atomically thin CDW systems is a novel direction. The investigation of how the collective CDW state and its fluctuations manifest in noise within a single, few-atom-wide chain has not been previously attempted.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Nanowire Geometry and Surface for Target Thermal Conductivity via a Coupled Phonon Transport and Electronic Noise Framework
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of nanoscale electronic and optoelectronic devices, such as the β-Ga2O3 diodes studied by Balandin's group, is critically limited by the thermal conductivity of constituent nanostructures. Traditional forward modeling predicts properties from a given geometry, but there is no systematic, physics-based inverse design methodology to engineer nanowire geometry and surface morphology to achieve a specific, application-driven thermal conductivity target, hindering the rational design of high-power-density devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by inverting a coupled phonon transport and low-frequency electronic noise model, we can deterministically design the diameter, length, and surface roughness profile of semiconductor nanowires to realize a pre-specified thermal conductivity, with the electronic noise serving as a sensitive probe of surface scattering effects.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse design algorithm based on a modified Boltzmann transport equation for phonons, incorporating diameter-dependent boundary scattering and a surface roughness model parameterized from atomic force microscopy. The forward model will be coupled with a 1/f noise model, informed by Balandin's prior work, linking surface state density to phonon scattering rates. The inversion will be performed using a Bayesian optimization framework, which will iteratively search the geometric parameter space (diameter, taper, roughness correlation length) to minimize the difference between the predicted and target thermal conductivity, validated by fabricating and testing designed Si and GaN nanowires.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-of-its-kind computational tool for the inverse thermal design of nanowires, directly linking geometric parameters to a macroscopic transport property. It will provide new fundamental insights into the coupling between surface morphology, phonon spectra, and electronic noise, advancing the field of nanoscale thermal engineering beyond predictive modeling towards targeted synthesis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing nanowire thermal design as a formal inverse problem and uniquely integrating electronic noise analysis as a feedback mechanism to refine the surface scattering model, moving beyond conventional trial-and-error or purely forward simulation approaches prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Engineering Ultra-High Thermal Resistance in Nanowires via Phonon Spectral Density and Quantum Composite Modeling
proposals.motivation: Efficient thermal insulation at the nanoscale is critical for next-generation electronics, thermoelectrics, and quantum devices, where heat dissipation limits performance and stability. Pre-2026 work has established that low-frequency noise spectroscopy and phonon engineering in materials like boron nitride and charge-density-wave systems provide deep insights into thermal transport bottlenecks. However, a predictive framework to deliberately maximize thermal resistance by designing nanowire structure and composition is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thermal conductivity of engineered nanowires can be minimized by combining spectral density analysis of phonon scattering with composite fillers that introduce strong phonon localization and Umklapp scattering, guided by a model integrating material, charge, and temperature-dependent electronic properties.
proposals.method: We will fabricate nanowires of β-(AlxGa1−x)2O3 and boron nitride composites, embedding charge-density-wave quantum fillers (e.g., 1T-TaS2) to create heterogeneous interfaces. Thermal resistance will be measured via micro-Raman thermometry and 3ω method across 10-500 K. The core model will use Boltzmann transport equations, parameterized by low-frequency noise data and acoustic phonon spectra from prior work, to iteratively optimize filler concentration, interface density, and alloy disorder to maximize phonon scattering cross-section.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a experimentally validated design principle for ultra-high thermal resistance nanowires, directly linking noise and phonon spectral signatures to macroscopic thermal properties. It will establish a new class of quantum-composite thermal insulators with tailored electronic-thermal coupling, advancing materials for energy-efficient diodes and thermally managed quantum devices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic use of low-frequency noise as a diagnostic for phonon scattering centers and the integration of charge-density-wave fillers—typically studied for electronic properties—as deliberate phonon-localizing agents within a predictive thermal engineering model.

proposals.title: Buried Thermal Quilts for In-Plane Heat Spreading in Wide-Bandgap Semiconductor Heterostructures
proposals.motivation: Effective heat dissipation in wide-bandgap power electronics (e.g., β-Ga2O3) is critical for reliability and performance, as self-heating degrades carrier mobility and increases low-frequency electronic noise. Conventional top-side thermal management competes for space with interconnects and gates. This proposal addresses the fundamental thermal bottleneck by exploring a paradigm shift from surface cooling to integrated, in-plane heat spreading within the device substrate itself.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a buried, engineered layer of thermally anisotropic quantum composite materials—acting as a 'thermal quilt'—within the substrate will significantly enhance in-plane heat spreading, reduce the peak channel temperature, and consequently lower the 1/f noise in active devices by mitigating local hot-spot formation.
proposals.method: We will fabricate heterostructures by embedding fillers with high in-plane thermal conductivity, such as exfoliated hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) flakes or charge-density-wave materials (e.g., 1T-TaS2), into an insulating matrix (e.g., polycrystalline AlN or SiO2) on diamond or high-quality cubic boron nitride substrates. β-(AlxGa1−x)2O3 Schottky diodes will be fabricated atop these buried thermal quilts. The thermal transport will be characterized via micro-Raman thermometry and transient thermoreflectance, while the electronic impact will be assessed through temperature-dependent current-voltage and low-frequency noise spectroscopy measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials integration strategy for in-substrate thermal management, directly linking engineered thermal anisotropy to improved electronic device metrics like noise. It will provide a foundational framework for co-designing thermal and electronic properties in next-generation ultra-wide-bandgap semiconductor technologies.
proposals.novelty: The approach is non-obvious as it inverts the standard thermal management paradigm by moving the primary heat-spreading element from the top surface to a buried interface, leveraging Balandin's expertise in quantum composites and charge-density-wave fillers for thermal, not just electronic, functionality.

proposals.title: Graphene Quilts for Thermal Management and Low-Noise Operation in Diamond and SiC High-Power Diodes
proposals.motivation: High-power semiconductor devices based on wide-bandgap materials like diamond and SiC face severe self-heating and elevated low-frequency (1/f) noise, which degrades performance and reliability. The graphene 'quilt' concept, pioneered for GaN, has shown promise in simultaneously improving heat spreading and suppressing noise by modulating charge-density-wave fluctuations. Extending this approach to other high-power material systems is a critical, unexplored frontier for next-generation electronics.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating patterned graphene quilts as fillers into the device architecture of diamond and 4H-SiC Schottky barrier diodes will significantly reduce the device thermal resistance and the magnitude of the 1/f noise by providing efficient lateral heat extraction and stabilizing charge transport through local screening effects.
proposals.method: We will fabricate Schottky diodes on single-crystal diamond and 4H-SiC substrates. Patterned multilayer graphene quilts will be transferred and embedded at the metal-semiconductor interface or within passivation layers. Device characterization will involve temperature-dependent current-voltage (I-V) and capacitance-voltage (C-V) measurements, low-frequency noise spectroscopy (from 1 Hz to 100 kHz), and lock-in thermography for direct thermal mapping. The noise and thermal data will be correlated with the quilt geometry and density.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials-agnostic framework for using 2D material quilts to co-optimize thermal and electronic performance in power devices. It will provide the first experimental dataset on noise suppression via graphene quilts in ultra-wide-bandgap semiconductors, directly informing the design of more reliable high-power electronics.
proposals.novelty: While graphene quilts have been studied for GaN, their application to diamond and SiC—materials with distinct phonon spectra and surface states—is novel. The combined investigation of their dual role in heat spreading and 1/f noise mitigation in these systems is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Atomic-Layer-Engineered Interfaces for Thermal Transport and Low-Frequency Noise Suppression in Ga2O3 and Quantum Composite Devices
proposals.motivation: Thermal management is a critical bottleneck in high-power electronics and quantum materials, where poor interfacial thermal conductance (ITC) limits performance and reliability. Pre-2026 work, including Balandin's studies on β-Ga2O3 diodes and quantum composites, identifies thermal interfaces as key sources of excess noise and heat accumulation. Precise control of interfacial phonon transport, however, remains a significant challenge for these emerging material systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ultrathin, conformal interfacial layers deposited via atomic layer deposition (ALD) will significantly enhance ITC by chemically passivating surfaces and providing a graded phonon density of states, thereby reducing thermal boundary resistance and suppressing low-frequency 1/f noise generated at material interfaces.
proposals.method: We will employ thermal time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR) to measure ITC across engineered interfaces in β-(AlxGa1−x)2O3 Schottky diodes and quantum composites with charge-density-wave fillers (e.g., 1T-TaS2). ALD will be used to deposit ultrathin (1-5 nm) layers of Al2O3, HfO2, and BN at critical interfaces. Low-frequency noise spectroscopy will correlate the measured ITC improvement with the suppression of 1/f noise in diode current and composite conductivity, linking thermal and electronic transport phenomena.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials-processing framework for phonon engineering at interfaces using ALD, directly applicable to wide-bandgap electronics and quantum material heterostructures. It will provide a quantitative link between engineered ITC and electronic noise, a key reliability metric, offering a new strategy for co-optimizing thermal and electronic performance.
proposals.novelty: While ALD is common for electronic passivation, its systematic application for phonon transport and noise control at interfaces in Ga2O3 devices and quantum composites is unexplored. The combined thermal and noise metrology approach directly connects interfacial phonon physics with device-level electronic phenomena.

proposals.title: Phase-Change Thermal Buffers for Noise Suppression and Hotspot Mitigation in Ga2O3 Power Diodes
proposals.motivation: Wide-bandgap semiconductors like β-Ga2O3 enable high-power electronics but suffer from localized self-heating and current filamentation, leading to increased low-frequency 1/f noise and premature device failure. Effective thermal management of transient heat pulses at microscopic hotspots remains a critical, unsolved challenge for device reliability. Phase-change materials (PCMs), used for bulk thermal energy storage, offer unexplored potential for localized, passive thermal buffering at the device level.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a thin-film PCM layer adjacent to the active region of a β-Ga2O3 Schottky barrier diode will absorb transient thermal pulses, reduce local temperature spikes, and consequently suppress low-frequency electronic noise and current density fluctuations.
proposals.method: We will fabricate β-Ga2O3 SBDs with a micro-patterned, conformal thin film of a low-phase-transition-temperature PCM (e.g., paraffin-based or salt hydrate) deposited near the Schottky contact. Device performance, low-frequency noise spectra, and current-voltage characteristics will be measured under pulsed electrical stress. Simultaneously, we will employ micro-Raman thermography to map the spatial and temporal temperature evolution at the hotspot with and without the PCM buffer, correlating thermal dynamics with noise metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel device-integrated thermal management strategy using PCMs, directly linking thermal buffering to electronic noise reduction. It will provide quantitative guidelines for selecting PCM properties (latent heat, transition temperature) for specific semiconductor devices, advancing the field of phonon engineering for electronics reliability.
proposals.novelty: While PCMs are used in building insulation, their application as an integrated, microscopic thermal buffer for active electronic devices to control noise is unprecedented. This approach synergizes Balandin's expertise in noise, thermal transport in wide-bandgap materials, and quantum composites with charge-density-wave fillers, which are analogous in their phase-transition behavior.

proposals.title: Ultrafast Laser Shaping of Graphene Quilts for Low-Noise Quantum Transport Devices
proposals.motivation: Graphene quilts, comprising interconnected graphene flakes, offer a promising platform for quantum transport and charge-density-wave phenomena, but their performance is critically limited by edge disorder and contamination from conventional lithography. Pre-2026 work has shown that edge quality governs low-frequency 1/f noise and electronic density fluctuations, which are central to Balandin's research themes. A method for shaping these materials with minimal damage is essential to probe their intrinsic quantum properties.
proposals.hypothesis: Femtosecond laser ablation, with its sub-picosecond material interaction time, can pattern graphene quilts with atomically clean edges, significantly reducing edge-defect-induced scattering and noise compared to techniques like plasma etching. This will lead to enhanced electronic coherence and measurable improvements in low-frequency noise metrics in quantum transport.
proposals.method: We will use a Ti:Sapphire femtosecond laser system (800 nm, <100 fs pulses) for direct-write patterning of CVD-grown and transferred graphene quilts on SiO2/Si and h-BN substrates. Laser fluence, scan speed, and repetition rate will be optimized via Raman spectroscopy mapping to minimize the D-peak intensity, indicating low defect creation. The patterned quilts will be fabricated into multi-terminal Hall bar devices with Cr/Au contacts. Their electronic performance will be characterized by low-temperature (4.2 K) measurements of magneto-resistance, quantum Hall states, and low-frequency current noise spectroscopy, directly comparing laser-patterned and plasma-etched control devices.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel nanofabrication protocol for 2D material heterostructures with pristine edges, directly impacting the field of quantum electronics. It will provide the first systematic study linking ultrafast laser patterning quality to the suppression of 1/f noise and the enhancement of quantum coherence in graphene-based composite systems, offering a new pathway for noise-optimized device engineering.
proposals.novelty: While femtosecond lasers are used for graphene cutting, their application to shape complex, multi-flake 'quilt' structures for quantum transport and noise reduction is unexplored. The novelty lies in integrating this precise machining technique with the specific materials system (graphene quilts) and performance metrics (low-frequency noise and quantum coherence) central to Balandin's expertise.

proposals.title: Engineering Phonon Transport via Designed Disorder in Quantum Dot Superlattices
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of quantum-confined electronic devices, such as ultra-scaled transistors and quantum dot (QD) lasers, is critically limited by poor heat dissipation. Traditional approaches seek to minimize disorder to maximize thermal conductivity, but this is often incompatible with the quantum confinement needed for electronic function. A paradigm shift is needed to actively use structural variations to guide, rather than simply impede, phonon flow.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically engineering the size, spacing, and compositional disorder in III-V or II-VI semiconductor QD superlattices, we can create preferential phonon transport channels that enhance directional thermal conduction, effectively inverting the traditional disorder analogy for thermal design.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of InAs/GaAs or CdSe/ZnSe QD superlattices with controlled, aperiodic variations in dot size and inter-dot spacing using molecular beam epitaxy. Thermal conductivity will be measured via the 3ω method and time-domain thermoreflectance as a function of temperature (10-300 K). Phonon spectra will be characterized using Raman and Brillouin spectroscopy. The experimental data will be compared to theoretical models combining Boltzmann transport equations with atomistic Green's function simulations to map the relationship between designed disorder profiles and the resulting phonon transmission spectra.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for thermal management in quantum-confined systems, demonstrating that disorder can be a tool rather than a detriment. It will provide a foundational materials platform for co-optimizing electronic quantum confinement and thermal transport, directly impacting the performance and reliability of nanoscale optoelectronics and quantum devices.
proposals.novelty: While disorder is universally considered detrimental to thermal conduction, this proposal deliberately engineers it to create a phononic analog of electronic percolation networks or charge-density-wave-like ordering for heat. This inverts the standard design rule and leverages Balandin's expertise in noise, quantum composites, and phonon engineering in novel materials.

proposals.title: Adversarial Model Validation for Disentangling Thermal Strain, Interdiffusion, and Barrier Effects on Superconducting Proximity in Quantum Composites
proposals.motivation: The performance of superconducting quantum devices and hybrid quantum composites critically depends on the transparency of material interfaces, which often degrades unpredictably with temperature. Pre-2026 work, including studies of charge-density-wave fillers and low-frequency noise in diodes, highlights the sensitivity of electronic transport to interfacial disorder and thermal effects, but a unified, predictive model for superconducting interface transparency is lacking. Identifying the dominant physical mechanism—be it thermal strain, chemical interdiffusion, or barrier formation—is essential for designing stable quantum hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: The temperature-dependent transparency of a superconducting interface is governed by a competition between discrete physical mechanisms (thermal strain, interdiffusion, barrier evolution), and their individual contributions can be quantified by adversarially validating a multi-physics computational model against a coordinated suite of in-situ experimental probes.
proposals.method: We will fabricate model interfaces using superconducting thin films on substrates with mismatched thermal expansion, informed by Balandin's expertise in phonon and noise characterization. A coordinated experimental suite will measure in-situ temperature-dependent Andreev reflection spectroscopy (transparency), low-frequency electronic noise (defect dynamics), X-ray diffraction (strain), and cross-sectional STEM/EDS (interdiffusion) on the same interface. A computational model will integrate these mechanisms using coupled finite-element analysis for strain and kinetic Monte Carlo for diffusion, with its predictions adversarially validated against the full dataset via a discriminator network that identifies inconsistencies, forcing model refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, multi-physics framework to predict interface transparency evolution, moving beyond phenomenological descriptions. It will establish a diagnostic protocol, combining noise and structural probes, to identify the dominant degradation mechanism in a given material system. This enables the rational design of quantum composites and superconducting circuits with interfaces robust across operational temperature ranges.
proposals.novelty: The adversarial validation of a physical model against a multi-faceted experimental dataset, rather than a single measurement, is a novel approach to isolate competing mechanisms. Integrating low-frequency noise spectroscopy—a Balandin group specialty—as a direct probe of interfacial defect dynamics for superconducting transparency is a unique and powerful strategy.

proposals.title: Temperature-Dependent Interface Transparency in Superconductor Heterostructures: An Analogy from Microelectronic Contact Physics
proposals.motivation: The transparency parameter at superconductor-normal metal (S-N) or superconductor-semiconductor (S-Sm) interfaces is a critical determinant of device performance in quantum computing and cryogenic electronics, yet it is almost universally treated as a temperature-independent constant in models like the Blonder-Tinkham-Klapwijk (BTK) theory. This simplification ignores potential thermal effects on scattering processes, gap profiles, and quasiparticle states, limiting the predictive accuracy of models for real devices operating across temperature ranges. Drawing a direct analogy to the well-established temperature dependence of metal-semiconductor contact resistance in microelectronics can provide a rigorous framework to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: The interface transparency in superconductor heterostructures exhibits a significant, measurable temperature dependence due to mechanisms analogous to those in semiconductor contacts, such as thermal smearing of states and phonon-assisted processes. This dependence can be systematically quantified by adapting microelectronic test structures and analysis methods to superconducting systems.
proposals.method: We will fabricate dedicated superconducting test junctions, analogous to microelectronic Transmission Line Model (TLM) patterns, using thin-film superconductors (e.g., Nb, Al) and normal metals (e.g., Cu, Au) or semiconductors (e.g., InAs). Low-frequency noise spectroscopy and temperature-dependent current-voltage (I-V) measurements will be performed from below Tc to above, isolating the interface contribution. The data will be fitted to a modified BTK model where the transparency parameter is an explicit function of temperature, incorporating terms for thermal broadening and potentially phonon-mediated tunneling, informed by models for thermionic emission in semiconductors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new experimental and theoretical methodology for characterizing superconductor interfaces, moving beyond the constant-transparency paradigm. It will provide crucial, quantitative data on thermal effects on interface transport, directly impacting the design and modeling of superconducting qubits, cryogenic transistors, and hybrid quantum materials. The successful adaptation of microelectronic concepts will create a novel cross-disciplinary link between these fields.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual transfer of temperature-dependent contact resistance models from mature semiconductor physics to the domain of superconductor interfaces, a connection not previously explored. The proposal to use TLM-like superconducting test structures and low-frequency noise measurements to deconvolve interface effects is a non-obvious, materials-focused experimental strategy.

proposals.title: Ultra-Sensitive Sub-10K Thermal Conductivity Measurements via Astrophysical-Inspired Lock-In Detection with Transition-Edge Sensors
proposals.motivation: Accurate thermal conductivity measurements below 10K are critical for testing fundamental limits of phonon and electron transport models, including boundary scattering and quantum effects, in emerging quantum materials and low-dimensional systems. However, conventional techniques suffer from poor signal-to-noise ratio due to the extreme weakness of thermal signals and parasitic background noise at these cryogenic temperatures, hindering precise model validation.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the signal-isolation techniques of far-infrared astrophysical detectors to a cryogenic thermal transport platform, we can achieve unprecedented measurement sensitivity, enabling the direct experimental verification of theoretical predictions for thermal conductivity in the sub-10K quantum regime.
proposals.method: We will design a measurement platform integrated into a dilution refrigerator with a base temperature below 100 mK. A microfabricated on-sample heater will apply a modulated (chopped) heat flux. The resulting temperature wave will be detected by a transition-edge sensor (TES) thermometer, whose output will be processed by a lock-in amplifier to isolate the signal from noise. The amplitude and phase delay of the temperature oscillation will be used to extract the thermal conductivity and diffusivity with high precision.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, high-sensitivity methodology for low-temperature thermal transport, directly testing the limits of existing phonon and electron transport models. It will provide benchmark data for advanced materials like charge-density-wave systems and wide-bandgap semiconductors, where low-frequency noise and quantum effects dominate thermal properties.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious adaptation lies in directly transferring the core signal recovery architecture—sub-Kelvin detector environment, modulated source, and lock-in detection—from the astrophysical problem of detecting cold interstellar dust to the condensed matter problem of measuring nanoscale thermal transport, a conceptual leap across disciplines.

proposals.title: Anisotropic Thermal Transport in Quantum Dot Superlattices with Charge-Density-Wave Fillers
proposals.motivation: Quantum dot (QD) superlattices are promising for thermoelectric and quantum optoelectronic applications, where thermal management is critical. However, the directional dependence (anisotropy) of their thermal conductivity remains poorly understood, especially when QDs are embedded in or coupled to correlated electron materials like charge-density-wave (CDW) systems, which can introduce unique phonon scattering and electronic thermal pathways. Pre-2026 work on CDW composites and low-noise devices highlights the importance of thermal and electronic properties in novel material systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thermal conductivity of QD superlattices with CDW fillers will exhibit strong anisotropy, with significantly lower conductivity perpendicular to the layers due to enhanced interface scattering and CDW-induced phonon suppression, and that this anisotropy can be electrically modulated via the CDW phase transition.
proposals.method: We will fabricate epitaxial QD superlattices (e.g., III-V or II-VI semiconductors) with controlled periodicity, interfacing them with CDW filler materials (e.g., 1T-TaS2) inspired by prior work on quantum composites. Thermal conductivity parallel (κ∥) and perpendicular (κ⊥) to the layers will be measured using microfabricated suspended platforms with integrated serpentine heaters and thermometers for in-plane measurements (3ω method) and time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR) for cross-plane measurements. Electronic transport and low-frequency noise spectroscopy will be employed concurrently to correlate thermal anisotropy with charge density and phase transitions in the CDW filler.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative map of thermal anisotropy in QD superlattice-CDWF composites, establishing a fundamental link between engineered nanostructure, correlated electron phases, and directional heat flow. It will deliver critical design rules for managing heat in anisotropic quantum materials for next-generation diodes and thermoelectric devices.
proposals.novelty: While thermal anisotropy in traditional superlattices is known, the integration of CDW fillers—materials with strong electron-phonon coupling and phase transitions—into QD superlattices to actively tune directional heat transport is a novel and unexplored approach, merging concepts from quantum composites with nanoscale thermal metrology.

proposals.title: Probing the Quantum Limits of Phonon Transport: Thermal Conductance of a Single Quantum Dot via Suspended Nanoscale Probes
proposals.motivation: Understanding thermal transport at the single-quantum-dot level is a fundamental challenge in nanoscale physics, with direct implications for quantum information processing and energy-efficient nanoelectronics. Prior to 2026, thermal measurements of nanostructures have been ensemble-averaged or inferred indirectly, obscuring the intrinsic quantum and coherent phonon effects that dominate at the single-dot scale. This work directly addresses this gap by proposing a direct measurement, connecting to Balandin's expertise in phonon engineering and low-noise characterization of quantum materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thermal conductance of a single semiconductor quantum dot will exhibit quantized steps at low temperatures due to the discrete spectrum of acoustic phonon modes available for transport, and that this quantization will be modulated by the quantum dot's electronic charge state via electron-phonon coupling.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a single InAs/GaAs quantum dot, or a precise chain of two dots, suspended between two released silicon nitride nanobeams acting as thermal probes/thermometers. Each nanobeam will incorporate a superconducting NbN microbolometer, optimized based on pre-2026 noise-mitigation techniques from Balandin's group, to measure temperature rises with nK sensitivity. Thermal conductance will be measured by applying a known Joule heating power to one beam and detecting the temperature difference in a 3ω-like differential measurement scheme, while simultaneously controlling the dot's charge state via a nearby nanofabricated gate and monitoring its electronic spectrum through embedded quantum point contacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental data on the fundamental phonon transport limits through a zero-dimensional system, quantifying the role of quantum confinement and electron-phonon coupling. It will establish a new experimental platform for studying energy dissipation in quantum devices and inform the design of quantum composites with charge-density-wave fillers by revealing the thermal interface at the single-filler level.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, *in-situ* thermal measurement of a *single* quantum object, moving beyond ensemble or substrate-mediated techniques, and the concurrent electrostatic control of its charge state to probe the interplay between electronic and thermal quantum transport.

proposals.title: Phonon Engineering in Three-Dimensional Quantum Dot Superlattices via Template-Directed Assembly: A Study of Topology-Dependent Thermal Transport
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of next-generation electronic and optoelectronic devices is critically limited by material interfaces and nanostructure disorder, which scatter phonons unpredictably. Pre-2026 work on low-frequency noise in wide-bandgap diodes and phonons in boron nitride films highlights the profound impact of material microstructure on electronic and thermal properties. A systematic method to create ordered 3D quantum dot (QD) arrays is needed to decouple geometric effects from material synthesis, enabling the rational design of thermal metamaterials with engineered, anisotropic, or ultralow thermal conductivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the long-range topological order and symmetry of a three-dimensional QD array, dictated by its scaffold template, will govern its phonon transport spectrum and thermal conductivity by introducing coherent and incoherent scattering boundaries that are tunable by design.
proposals.method: We will fabricate 3D porous scaffolds with defined lattice geometries (simple cubic, face-centered cubic, diamond) using two-photon polymerization lithography and atomic layer deposition of alumina. Colloidal QDs (e.g., PbS, CdSe) will be infiltrated into the scaffolds using electrophoretic deposition and ligand-exchange binding to ensure uniform filling. The thermal conductivity of the composite QD arrays will be characterized using micro-Raman thermometry and the 3ω method, correlating measured values with array topology and inter-dot spacing. Low-frequency noise spectroscopy will be employed to probe charge trapping and phonon interactions at the QD-scaffold interfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental design rules linking 3D QD array topology to thermal transport, moving beyond disordered composites. It will provide a novel material platform for testing theories of phonon confinement and Anderson localization in engineered 3D potentials. The results will directly inform the development of quantum composites with tailored thermal properties for thermoelectrics and thermal barrier coatings.
proposals.novelty: While 2D QD arrays and disordered QD composites have been studied, the template-directed assembly of 3D QD superlattices with precise topological control for thermal transport engineering is a novel approach. This decouples the challenges of QD synthesis and 3D geometric patterning, a significant advance over prior methods.

proposals.title: Three-Dimensional Quantum Dot Phononic Crystals for Anisotropy-Free Thermal Transport Management
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of advanced electronic and optoelectronic devices is a critical bottleneck, exacerbated by the anisotropic thermal conductivity of many emerging low-dimensional and layered materials. Pre-2026 work on quantum dot (QD) arrays has largely focused on 2D configurations for electronic or optical properties, neglecting their potential as engineered phononic media for controlling heat flow in three dimensions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fabricating isotropic, three-dimensional arrays of quantum dots will create a phononic crystal with a tunable, direction-independent thermal conductivity, governed by the coherent scattering of acoustic phonons from the periodic QD lattice, thereby enabling novel thermal management paradigms.
proposals.method: The project will fabricate 3D QD arrays using layer-by-layer assembly of colloidal PbSe or CdSe QDs, with organic/inorganic surface ligands to control spacing, followed by cross-linking to form a robust, isotropic scaffold. Structural characterization will employ TEM and XRD. Thermal transport properties will be measured via time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR) and the 3ω method across a 10-500 K temperature range. Low-frequency noise spectroscopy, a core expertise of the PI, will be used to probe charge carrier interactions with the modified phonon spectrum in test diode structures embedded with the 3D QD composite.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational materials platform for isotropic thermal conductivity engineering via 3D phononic band structure design. It will provide the first experimental data set linking 3D QD superlattice geometry to macroscopic thermal transport, directly impacting the design of heat spreaders and thermoelectrics. The integration of noise spectroscopy will offer new insights into electron-phonon coupling in these complex quantum composites.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the shift from electronic to *phononic* engineering using 3D QD arrays and the pursuit of isotropic, rather than anisotropic, thermal properties. Combining 3D nanofabrication with established noise characterization techniques for thermal analysis presents a unique, non-obvious methodology.

proposals.title: Multi-Scale Thermal Impedance Spectroscopy of Quantum Dot Ensembles: A Non-Destructive Probe for Size Dispersion and Interface Disorder
proposals.motivation: The thermal properties of quantum dot (QD) ensembles are critical for thermoelectric and optoelectronic applications, yet they are governed by a complex interplay of intrinsic QD phonon spectra, interface scattering, and film morphology. Pre-2026 techniques like transient thermoreflectance provide an effective thermal conductivity but struggle to deconvolute these multi-scale scattering contributions quantitatively. A method that can separately characterize QD core properties and interface quality is needed to guide material synthesis and device optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: The complex thermal impedance spectrum of a QD film, measured under an oscillatory temperature gradient, contains distinct frequency-dependent features that can be uniquely mapped to specific scattering length scales, enabling the simultaneous extraction of QD size distribution metrics and interfacial thermal conductance.
proposals.method: We will adapt a 3ω method platform to perform thermal impedance spectroscopy (TIS). A metallic heater/thermometer line patterned on a suspended membrane will apply a sinusoidal heat flux (frequency f) to a deposited QD film. The complex thermal impedance Z_th(f) = ΔT(f)/P(f) will be measured from 10 Hz to 100 kHz. The spectrum will be fitted to an equivalent thermal R-C network model, where resistors represent thermal resistances (QD cores, interfaces, film boundaries) and capacitors represent thermal capacitances of constituent layers. A genetic algorithm will be used to optimize model parameters, including a distribution of core resistances linked to a log-normal QD size distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish TIS as a novel, non-destructive metrology tool for nanostructured materials, providing a single measurement that yields quantitative metrics for both QD monodispersity and interfacial disorder. It will create a new experimental link between the statistical structural properties of an ensemble and its frequency-domain thermal response, advancing the understanding of phonon transport in disordered systems.
proposals.novelty: While the 3ω method measures thermal conductivity, applying impedance analysis concepts from electrochemistry to deconvolute thermal transport into length-scale-specific contributions is a novel analogy. This transforms a standard thermal measurement into a powerful spectroscopic tool for structural characterization.

proposals.title: Thermal Conductivity as a Probe of Quantum Dot Ensemble Disorder and Interface Quality in Charge-Density-Wave Composites
proposals.motivation: The performance of quantum dot (QD) and charge-density-wave (CDW) composite materials for electronic and quantum applications is critically limited by structural disorder, including QD size distribution and interface quality, which are difficult to characterize non-destructively. Pre-2026 work has established that thermal transport in nanostructures is exquisitely sensitive to scattering from boundaries and defects, yet this sensitivity has not been systematically leveraged to quantify ensemble-level structural parameters in functional composites.
proposals.hypothesis: The low-temperature thermal conductivity of a QD/CDW composite film will exhibit a quantifiable signature—specifically in the temperature dependence and magnitude of the phonon mean free path—that can be directly correlated with the statistical distribution of QD sizes and the degree of interfacial disorder between the QDs and the matrix.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of epitaxial thin-film composites with embedded QDs (e.g., 1T-TaS2 CDW fillers in a wide-bandgap matrix) where QD size and interface roughness are varied controllably during growth. Thermal conductivity (κ) will be measured from 2 K to 300 K using a steady-state, micro-fabricated suspended device method with integrated thermometers and heaters, ensuring high sensitivity to phonon scattering regimes. The experimental κ(T) data will be analyzed using a modified Callaway model, where the scattering rates for boundary, impurity, and interface scattering are parameterized by the QD size distribution (extracted from TEM) and an interfacial disorder factor, which will be iteratively optimized via a least-squares fitting algorithm to achieve a self-consistent structural descriptor.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish thermal transport measurement as a quantitative, non-destructive metrology for ensemble structural properties in nanocomposites, complementing direct imaging techniques. It will provide new insights into how interfacial disorder governs phonon and, by extension, charge carrier dynamics in quantum composite materials, directly informing the design of low-noise electronic and efficient thermoelectric devices.
proposals.novelty: While thermal conductivity is commonly used to study bulk defects, its deliberate use as a calibrated probe to deconvolve the specific contributions of QD size distribution and interface quality in a single measurement represents a novel, inverse problem approach to materials characterization.

proposals.title: Coupled Electron-Phonon Transport and Low-Frequency Noise in Quantum Dot Superlattices for High-ZT Thermoelectrics
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of thermoelectric energy conversion is governed by the dimensionless figure of merit ZT, which requires high electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient but low thermal conductivity. Quantum dot (QD) superlattices are promising for this due to their potential for phonon scattering and quantum-confinement-modified electron transport. However, pre-2026 work has largely studied electronic and thermal transport separately, leaving the coupled dynamics and its stability—critical for device applications—poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineered QD superlattices with charge-density-wave (CDW) fillers will enable strong phonon scattering to reduce lattice thermal conductivity while maintaining favorable electronic transport via miniband formation, and that the coupled transport will exhibit characteristic low-frequency noise signatures correlating with thermoelectric efficiency and stability.
proposals.method: We will fabricate QD superlattices using colloidal synthesis of lead chalcogenide (PbSe/PbS) nanocrystals, with intercalation of CDW materials (e.g., 1T-TaS2) as fillers to modify electron-phonon coupling. Electronic transport (conductivity, Seebeck coefficient) will be measured via a custom micro-device with variable temperature (10-400 K) and controlled charge injection. Simultaneously, thermal conductivity will be measured using the 3ω method on the same devices. Low-frequency (1/f) noise spectroscopy will be performed concurrently to probe defect dynamics and coupling mechanisms. The electronic band structure and phonon dispersion will be modeled using density functional theory (DFT) and Boltzmann transport equations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, experimental correlation between coupled electron-phonon transport properties and low-frequency noise in QD superlattices, establishing noise as a diagnostic tool for thermoelectric material quality. It will yield a new composite material paradigm integrating CDW fillers to independently tailor electronic and thermal transport channels, advancing the design of high-ZT systems.
proposals.novelty: The simultaneous measurement of thermoelectric coefficients and 1/f noise on the same QD superlattice device is novel, as is the integration of CDW quantum materials as active fillers to manipulate electron-phonon coupling beyond simple boundary scattering.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Dominant Phonon Scattering Mechanisms in High-Thermal-Conductivity Quantum Composites and Wide-Bandgap Semiconductors
proposals.motivation: The thermal conductivity of advanced electronic materials, such as wide-bandgap semiconductors and quantum composites, is critical for device performance and reliability. While isotopic disorder is often considered a primary phonon scattering mechanism limiting thermal transport, its relative importance compared to other sources like defects, interfaces, and electron-phonon coupling in modern materials is not fully quantified. This knowledge gap hinders the rational design of materials for high-power electronics and thermal management.
proposals.hypothesis: In engineered quantum composites and structurally complex wide-bandgap semiconductors, phonon scattering from extended defects, interfaces, and charge-density-wave fluctuations can be more limiting to thermal conductivity than isotopic mass disorder alone.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of β-(AlxGa1−x)2O3 thin films and quantum composites with charge-density-wave (CDW) fillers (e.g., 1T-TaS2) with controlled isotopic purity (using enriched 10B, 11B, 69Ga, 71Ga) and varying defect densities. Thermal conductivity will be measured via time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR) from 80K to 500K. Low-frequency electronic noise spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy will probe defect/interface and electron-phonon coupling effects. The experimental data will be analyzed using the Callaway model and first-principles Boltzmann transport equation (BTE) calculations to deconvolute the scattering rates from isotopes, point defects, boundaries, and CDW fluctuations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative hierarchy of phonon scattering mechanisms in two critical material systems, directly testing the limits of the isotope scattering paradigm. It will establish design rules for thermal management in next-generation high-power diodes and quantum composite electronics by identifying the primary scattering source to target for material optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the combined use of isotopic engineering, noise spectroscopy, and thermal transport modeling in quantum composite systems, moving beyond the study of simple elemental crystals to test the dominance of isotope scattering in structurally and electronically complex materials relevant to modern devices.

proposals.title: Disentangling Intrinsic and Extrinsic Phonon Scattering: A Noise Spectroscopy Study of Isotopic Boron Nitride on Engineered Substrates
proposals.motivation: The thermal conductivity isotope effect is a key signature of intrinsic phonon-phonon scattering, but its experimental quantification in thin films is confounded by extrinsic scattering from defects, interfaces, and substrates. Pre-2026, the assumption of purely intrinsic behavior in isotope-enriched materials like boron nitride (BN) limits the accurate extraction of fundamental phonon properties. This work addresses the critical need to isolate these contributions to validate theoretical models of thermal transport in quantum and wide-bandgap materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the measured isotopic effect on thermal conductivity in thin-film BN is significantly modulated by extrinsic phonon scattering at the film-substrate interface, and that low-frequency electronic noise in integrated devices can serve as a sensitive, local probe of this interfacial thermal coupling.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of isotopically enriched (10B, 11B) turbostratic and cubic BN thin films, following Balandin's established methods, on substrates with systematically varied acoustic impedance (diamond, sapphire, SiC). Thermal conductivity will be measured via micro-Raman thermometry and time-domain thermoreflectance. Concurrently, we will fabricate metal-BN-metal test structures and Schottky barrier diodes to measure low-frequency 1/f noise as a function of applied current and temperature, correlating noise magnitude with substrate-induced thermal boundary resistance and phonon leakage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, multi-modal framework for deconvolving intrinsic and extrinsic thermal transport mechanisms in isotopic materials. It will yield quantitative guidelines for substrate engineering to access the true intrinsic isotope effect and establish low-frequency noise as a diagnostic tool for interfacial phonon dynamics, with implications for thermal management in electronics and quantum composites.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the combined use of noise spectroscopy and thermal measurements to directly probe substrate-mediated extrinsic scattering, moving beyond the standard assumption of intrinsic-only behavior in isotope effect studies. This approach leverages Balandin's unique expertise in both phonon engineering and electronic noise.

proposals.title: Phonon Engineering and Low-Frequency Noise in Isotopically Patterned Graphene Superlattices
proposals.motivation: Phonon scattering governs thermal and electronic transport in 2D materials, directly impacting device performance and noise characteristics. Pre-2026 work has shown isotopic enrichment (e.g., 13C) modifies thermal conductivity in graphene, but creating periodic isotopic superlattices—artificial phononic crystals at the atomic scale—remains unexplored. This offers a novel pathway to control phonon spectra, thermal transport, and the associated electronic noise, a core theme in Balandin's research on material noise and quantum composites.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that periodic isotopic patterning in graphene will create minibands and stopgaps for acoustic phonons, enabling precise engineering of phonon-mediated processes, which will manifest as tunable thermal conductivity and suppressed generation-recombination noise in electronic transport measurements.
proposals.method: We will synthesize isotopically patterned graphene superlattices via chemical vapor deposition (CVD) using spatially controlled pulses of 12CH4 and 13CH4 precursor gases. Structural characterization will employ Raman spectroscopy and scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM). Phonon dispersion will be probed using micro-Raman spectroscopy and ultrafast optical pump-probe techniques.
