Electronic and noise properties will be measured by fabricating field-effect transistors (FETs) and Schottky barrier diodes from the superlattices, conducting low-frequency (1/f) noise spectroscopy as a function of temperature and charge density.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental platform for atomic-scale phononic crystals via isotopic patterning, directly linking engineered phonon band structures to electronic device metrics. It will provide new fundamental insights into phonon scattering and generation-recombination noise mechanisms in 2D materials, advancing the field of phonon engineering for thermal management and low-noise electronics.
proposals.novelty: While isotopic enrichment is known, the concept of a periodic isotopic superlattice within a single, continuous graphene sheet for phonon band engineering is novel. This approach uniquely merges atomic-level material design with macroscopic noise and thermal transport measurement, a synthesis not yet attempted in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Substrate-Engineered Enhancement of Flexural Phonon Transport and Thermal Conductivity in van der Waals Heterostructures
proposals.motivation: In electronic and optoelectronic devices, the substrate is traditionally viewed as a source of detrimental phonon scattering, degrading thermal conductivity and increasing low-frequency noise. However, recent work on phonon spectra in boron nitride films on diamond suggests substrates can modify acoustic phonon dispersion. This proposal aims to fundamentally re-evaluate the substrate's role, leveraging it as an active design element to control thermal transport, which is critical for power electronics and quantum composites where heat dissipation limits performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a strategically patterned or nanostructured substrate can constructively interfere with the flexural (out-of-plane) acoustic phonon modes of a two-dimensional (2D) or thin-film material, reducing scattering and thereby enhancing the cross-plane thermal conductivity of the heterostructure.
proposals.method: We will fabricate van der Waals heterostructures by transferring exfoliated flakes of graphene and hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) onto silicon substrates with engineered surface phononic crystals (periodic nanopillar arrays). Thermal conductivity will be measured using micro-Raman thermometry and the optothermal technique. Low-frequency noise spectroscopy will be employed to probe the interaction between charge carriers and the modified phonon spectrum. Theoretical support will involve solving the Boltzmann transport equation with substrate-imposed boundary conditions derived from finite-element modeling of phonon dispersion.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for thermal management by demonstrating a substrate that actively improves, rather than degrades, thermal transport. It will provide a direct experimental link between engineered phonon dispersion and measurable enhancements in thermal conductivity and noise characteristics, offering new design rules for high-power diodes and quantum composite materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the conventional wisdom of the substrate as a purely parasitic element. Instead of minimizing substrate interaction, we deliberately engineer it to create a favorable phononic environment, a concept not explored in prior thermal management or noise studies for 2D materials and thin films.

proposals.title: Substrate-Modified Negative Thermal Expansion in Supported Graphene: A Phonon-Based Model
proposals.motivation: Freestanding graphene exhibits a negative coefficient of thermal expansion (NTE) at low temperatures due to anharmonic phonon effects, a property crucial for thermal management in nanoelectronics. However, practical graphene devices are often supported on substrates or integrated into heterostructures, which impose strain and clamping forces that fundamentally alter its vibrational spectrum and thermal properties. Quantifying this substrate-induced modification is essential for designing reliable graphene-based transistors, diodes, and quantum composites where thermal-mechanical stability impacts electronic noise and charge transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that substrate-induced strain and clamping in graphene will significantly modify its low-frequency acoustic phonon modes, leading to a tunable transition from negative to positive thermal expansion, which can be quantitatively modeled by extending the existing NTE theory to include strain-modified Grüneisen parameters.
proposals.method: We will develop a semi-analytical model based on the Grüneisen formalism, where the key anharmonic phonon contributions are calculated from a modified valence force field potential that incorporates substrate-induced strain as a tunable parameter. The phonon dispersion and mode-specific Grüneisen parameters will be computed using density functional perturbation theory (DFPT) for graphene under varying biaxial strain conditions (0% to 2%). The thermal expansion coefficient will then be derived by integrating the strain-dependent phonon spectra. The model will be validated against existing experimental data for graphene on hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) and SiO2 substrates from pre-2026 literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first predictive, phonon-based model for the thermal expansion of supported graphene, directly linking substrate engineering to thermo-mechanical stability. It will deliver quantitative guidelines for selecting substrates and strain levels to either preserve NTE for stress compensation or induce positive expansion, thereby informing the design of graphene-based electronic and quantum devices with enhanced thermal reliability.
proposals.novelty: While NTE in freestanding graphene is known, a predictive model for how substrate interaction—a ubiquitous condition in devices—alters this fundamental property is lacking. This proposal uniquely integrates strain-tunable phonon physics, a core expertise of the PI in materials like boron nitride, into the established NTE framework for graphene.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of 2D Quantum Composites for Target Coefficients of Thermal Expansion via Flexural Mode Engineering
proposals.motivation: Precise control of the coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) is critical for high-performance electronics and optoelectronics to mitigate thermal stress and improve reliability. While materials like β-Ga2O3 show promise for power devices, their thermal management is challenging. The discovery of flexural-mode-driven negative thermal expansion (NTE) in 2D materials like graphene provides a new mechanism for CTE tuning, but a systematic, inverse design framework to create materials with a predetermined CTE is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: By computationally engineering the composition, stacking order, and interlayer coupling in van der Waals heterostructures and quantum composites, we can selectively control the population and coupling of low-energy flexural phonon modes to achieve a target CTE, including near-zero or negative values.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to calculate phonon spectra and Grüneisen parameters for candidate 2D materials (e.g., h-BN, graphene, MoS2) and their heterostructures. A genetic algorithm will be used to solve the inverse problem, optimizing layer sequence, twist angles, and the integration of charge-density-wave fillers (inspired by Balandin's prior work) to tailor the flexural mode density. The designed structures will be synthesized via chemical vapor deposition and characterized using temperature-dependent Raman spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction to validate CTE.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel computational-experimental pipeline for the inverse design of thermal expansion properties in 2D materials. It will yield specific material recipes for composites with engineered CTE, directly addressing thermal mismatch issues in wide-bandgap semiconductor devices. The project will also advance the fundamental understanding of phonon engineering in quantum composites.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying an inverse design paradigm—common in photonics but rare in thermal property engineering—specifically to harness flexural phonons for CTE control in 2D quantum composites, moving beyond serendipitous discovery to targeted material creation.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Graphene's Thermal Conductivity via Defect, Isotope, and Strain Engineering
proposals.motivation: Graphene's ultra-high thermal conductivity is crucial for next-generation nanoelectronics and quantum devices, but its extreme sensitivity to defects, isotopes, and strain makes precise engineering challenging. Current forward models predict conductivity from a given structure, but the inverse problem—determining the required structural profile to achieve a target conductivity—remains unsolved, hindering the rational design of thermal management materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of Boltzmann transport equation (BTE) solvers and Bayesian optimization can robustly solve the inverse design problem, mapping a target thermal conductivity in graphene to a specific, realizable configuration of point defects, isotope distributions, and strain profiles.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational framework coupling first-principles calculations of phonon properties with BTE solvers to generate a forward model database. A Bayesian optimization algorithm, using Gaussian processes, will then iteratively search the multi-dimensional parameter space (defect type/density, 13C isotope patterning, uniaxial/biaxial strain) to minimize the difference between the calculated and target conductivity. The approach will be validated against experimental measurements on fabricated samples using Raman thermometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first generalizable inverse-design methodology for tuning graphene's thermal conductivity, providing a critical tool for thermal management in 2D material-based devices. It will establish quantitative relationships between complex structural perturbations and phonon transport, advancing the field of nanoscale thermal engineering.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of thermal conductivity in graphene is established, the inverse problem is largely unexplored. The novel integration of BTE solvers with Bayesian optimization to design specific defect/strain landscapes for a thermal target represents a significant paradigm shift from characterization-by-chance to design-by-objective.

proposals.title: Universal Logarithmic Size Dependence of Low-Frequency 1/f Noise in Two-Dimensional Materials
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Balandin's group and others established that the normalized 1/f noise in graphene follows a unique logarithmic dependence on device area, a signature of its two-dimensionality and charge-number fluctuations. However, it remains unknown if this scaling law is a fundamental property of all 2D materials or a peculiarity of graphene's gapless, linear band structure. Testing this universality is critical for developing a general theory of electronic noise in 2D systems and for the reliable design of 2D-material-based electronic and sensor devices.
proposals.hypothesis: The logarithmic dependence of the normalized 1/f noise power on the device area, S_I/I^2 ∝ ln(1/A), is a universal feature of metallic and semiconducting two-dimensional crystals, arising from intrinsic carrier density fluctuations constrained to the 2D plane.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of Hall-bar and van der Pauw devices of systematically varying areas (from ~1 μm² to ~10⁴ μm²) from mechanically exfoliated flakes of diverse 2D materials: semiconducting MoS₂ and WS₂, metallic 1T-TaS₂ (with its charge-density-wave state), and few-layer hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN). Low-frequency noise spectra (1 Hz – 100 kHz) will be measured under controlled vacuum conditions as a function of charge carrier density (via back-gating), temperature (80–500 K), and current. The noise magnitude scaling with area will be extracted and fit to logarithmic and power-law models to determine universality.
proposals.contribution: This work will either establish a universal scaling law for noise in 2D materials, fundamentally linking mesoscopic fluctuations to dimensionality, or reveal material-specific deviations, pinpointing the roles of bandgap, defects, and many-body interactions. It will provide critical design rules for low-noise 2D electronics and validate theoretical models of 2D charge dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While the logarithmic scaling is well-documented for graphene, its systematic experimental investigation across a spectrum of electronically distinct 2D crystals—particularly those with correlated states like charge-density-waves—has not been performed, making the potential universality a non-trivial and open question.

proposals.title: Engineering Anomalous Thermal Transport in van der Waals Heterostructures via Twist-Angle and Stacking-Order Control
proposals.motivation: In most two-dimensional (2D) materials, thermal conductivity decreases with increasing layer number due to enhanced phonon-boundary and interlayer scattering, limiting heat dissipation in ultra-thin electronics. This trend poses a fundamental bottleneck for thermal management in next-generation devices based on layered materials. Inverting this trend would enable novel design principles for high-conductivity thermal interface materials and substrates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific interlayer twist angles and stacking sequences in select van der Waals heterostructures can create constructive phonon interference or suppress scattering channels, leading to an increase in cross-plane thermal conductivity with the addition of layers.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental approach. First, we will use density functional theory (DFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to screen candidate systems (e.g., graphene/h-BN, twisted bilayer MoS2, turbostratic BN) for stacking-dependent phonon spectra and scattering rates. Experimentally, we will fabricate heterostructures with controlled twist angles using deterministic transfer techniques. The thermal conductivity will be measured using micro-Raman thermometry and optothermal techniques, correlating results with structural characterization (TEM) and low-frequency noise spectroscopy to probe defect and interface quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials-by-design principle for controlling heat flow in layered systems, directly challenging the conventional understanding of layer-dependent thermal transport. It will provide a library of stacking configurations that exhibit anomalous thermal conductivity trends, with implications for thermal management in 2D electronics and quantum devices.
proposals.novelty: While twist-angle engineering is known for electronic properties (e.g., magic-angle graphene), its systematic application to deliberately invert the canonical layer-dependent thermal conductivity trend is unexplored. The proposal uniquely bridges Balandin's expertise in phonon engineering, noise spectroscopy, and thermal properties of layered materials like BN.

proposals.title: Intrinsic versus Substrate-Modified Properties of Few-Layer Graphene: A Direct, Layer-Dependent Quantification via On-Flake Suspended and hBN-Supported Reference Channels
proposals.motivation: The intrinsic electronic and thermal properties of few-layer graphene (FLG) are often obscured by substrate interactions, including charge inhomogeneity, phonon scattering, and strain. While suspended devices are assumed to reveal intrinsic behavior, a systematic, quantitative decoupling of substrate effects has been lacking, leading to significant discrepancies in reported values for mobility and thermal conductivity. This study directly addresses this fundamental materials science problem by providing a controlled, side-by-side comparison on a single flake.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the substrate's influence on carrier mobility, thermal conductivity, and phonon spectra in FLG is not uniform but depends critically on layer number and the specific property measured, and that an atomically flat hBN substrate will suppress charge disorder but still impose a measurable phonon scattering boundary compared to the suspended case.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a dual-measurement platform from a single, continuous mechanically exfoliated FLG flake. Using pre-2026 dry transfer and etching techniques, one segment will be fully suspended over a predefined trench, while an adjacent segment will be supported on exfoliated hexagonal boron nitride (hBN). Identical metal contacts will be deposited across both regions. Layer number will be confirmed via atomic force microscopy and optical contrast. Simultaneous four-probe DC and low-frequency noise measurements will quantify carrier mobility and disorder. Micro-Raman thermometry, using the G-peak shift as a local thermometer, will measure thermal conductivity. All measurements will be performed as a function of layer number (1 to 5) and temperature (80K to 400K).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first definitive, internally controlled map separating intrinsic FLG properties from substrate-induced modifications for mobility, thermal transport, and phonon behavior. It will establish quantitative correction factors for interpreting data from supported devices and clarify the role of layer-dependent substrate coupling in 2D material performance, directly impacting the design of future electronic and thermal management devices.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the on-flake reference design, which eliminates sample-to-sample variability by comparing suspended and supported properties on the same crystal with identical contacts, enabling a precise, layer-resolved deconvolution of substrate effects that has not been possible with separate device studies.

proposals.title: Isolating Intrinsic Electronic Properties of Few-Layer Graphene via a Suspended, Contact-Controlled Platform
proposals.motivation: The electronic properties of few-layer graphene (FLG), including carrier mobility, quantum capacitance, and low-frequency 1/f noise, are critical for next-generation nanoelectronics and quantum devices. However, reported measurements are highly inconsistent, as they are confounded by uncontrolled variables: the strong dielectric screening and phonon coupling from diverse substrates (SiO2, hBN, etc.) and the variable geometry and resistance of metallic contacts. This lack of a standardized measurement platform prevents the isolation of intrinsic, layer-dependent electronic behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by eliminating substrate dielectric effects and standardizing contact geometry, the measured electronic and noise properties of suspended FLG devices will reveal intrinsic, layer-dependent trends fundamentally different from those observed in substrate-supported devices, directly testing the assumption that substrate effects are negligible.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a platform of identical, narrow trenches on Si/SiO2 chips. Mechanically exfoliated FLG flakes (1 to 5 layers, identified by Raman spectroscopy) will be transferred to span these trenches. Using electron-beam lithography, we will pattern identical Ti/Au (5/50 nm) metal contacts in a Hall bar geometry directly onto the suspended portion of each flake. This creates a series of devices where only the layer count varies. We will perform temperature-dependent (4.2K to 300K) electronic transport and low-frequency 1/f noise spectroscopy measurements, applying our established analysis for noise magnitude and mobility extraction, to compare intrinsic properties across layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first controlled dataset of intrinsic electronic and noise properties for FLG, decoupled from substrate and contact variability. It will establish a definitive experimental benchmark for theoretical models of layer-dependent charge transport and noise in 2D materials, and validate a novel platform for studying other 2D systems in an isolated, 'in vitro' state.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, biochemistry-inspired 'reconstitution' approach: engineering a controlled experimental 'buffer' (suspension) and 'assay' (identical contacts) to isolate intrinsic material properties, a paradigm not previously applied to 2D material characterization. This directly challenges the entrenched assumption that substrate effects are a minor perturbation in FLG.

proposals.title: Seismic Tomography-Inspired Deconvolution of Phonon Scattering Mechanisms in Few-Layer Graphene from Temperature-Dependent Thermal Conductivity
proposals.motivation: Understanding the temperature-dependent thermal conductivity (κ) of 2D materials like few-layer graphene (FLG) is crucial for thermal management in nanoelectronics. However, standard κ(T) measurements provide only a lumped parameter, obscuring the individual contributions of competing phonon scattering mechanisms (e.g., boundary, defect, Umklapp). A method to deconvolve these contributions is needed to guide material engineering for optimal thermal performance across operational temperature ranges.
proposals.hypothesis: The temperature-dependent thermal conductivity of FLG can be analyzed using an inverse modeling approach, analogous to seismic tomography, to quantitatively map the temperature-dependent scattering rates and dominance of specific phonon scattering mechanisms as a function of layer number.
proposals.method: We will fabricate suspended FLG devices with controlled layer counts (1-5 layers) and measure κ(T) from 10K to 300K using micro-Raman thermometry or optothermal techniques. The κ(T) data will be fitted using a modified Callaway model, treating the total thermal resistivity as a sum of terms from distinct scattering processes. A constrained, regularized least-squares inversion algorithm—inspired by geophysical seismic tomography methods used to separate wave speed anomalies—will be employed to deconvolve the individual scattering strength parameters (e.g., boundary scattering length, defect density, Umklapp coefficient) as continuous functions of temperature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, temperature-resolved 'map' of dominant thermal resistance processes in FLG, moving beyond phenomenological fitting. It will establish a novel diagnostic framework for analyzing thermal transport in 2D materials, directly linking measured κ(T) to actionable structural and defect parameters. The methodology will be applicable to other layered materials and heterostructures.
proposals.novelty: The conceptual and methodological analogy to geophysical seismic tomography for deconvolving phonon scattering mechanisms in 2D materials is entirely novel. It transforms a standard transport measurement into an inverse problem to extract microscopic scattering landscapes, a significant departure from conventional forward-model fitting approaches in the field.

proposals.title: Causal Isolation of Substrate-Induced Phonon Scattering, Strain, and Doping Effects on Few-Layer Graphene Thermal Conductivity
proposals.motivation: Thermal management in 2D-material-based electronics is critical, yet the dramatic reduction of thermal conductivity (κ) in supported versus suspended flakes is poorly understood mechanistically. Pre-2026 studies show strong correlations but cannot disentangle the concurrent effects of interfacial scattering, substrate-induced strain, and charge transfer doping, hindering the rational design of thermal interfaces for 2D devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the substrate's impact on FLG thermal transport can be decomposed into three independent, additive contributions: (1) phonon scattering at the interface, (2) strain-induced modification of the phonon dispersion, and (3) carrier-phonon coupling from electrostatic doping, with the first being the dominant factor for common dielectric substrates.
proposals.method: We will fabricate suspended FLG devices with embedded Joule heaters and thermometers for baseline κ measurement via the 3ω method. Using a nano-positioning stage, we will controllably bring a selected substrate (SiO2, hBN, diamond) into contact while monitoring κ. We will then independently apply uniaxial strain via a piezoelectric actuator and electrostatic doping via a back-gate, measuring κ at each step. Simultaneous Raman spectroscopy will quantify strain and doping, while scanning thermal microscopy will map interfacial thermal conductance. A Boltzmann transport model will be fitted to the decoupled experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, causal model separating substrate effects on 2D material thermal transport. It will yield definitive metrics for substrate selection and interface engineering to optimize κ in 2D devices. The methodology establishes a general framework for mechanistic thermal analysis of supported van der Waals materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the sequential, active decoupling of three intertwined physical mechanisms within a single device, moving beyond passive comparison of different samples. The integrated in-situ measurement of thermal, spectroscopic, and electrostatic properties during controlled perturbation is a significant advance over static characterization.

proposals.title: Multi-Property Scaling Analysis of the 2D-to-3D Crossover in Few-Layer Graphene: Thermal, Electrical, and Optical Mapping
proposals.motivation: The layer-dependent evolution of properties in few-layer graphene (FLG) from 2D to 3D behavior is a fundamental materials physics problem with direct implications for nanoelectronic and thermoelectric device design. Prior studies, often limited to single properties like thermal conductivity for up to four layers, provide an incomplete picture, analogous to the historical challenge of characterizing the unentangled-to-entangled crossover in polymer melts using a single dynamic measurement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dimensional crossover in FLG is not a single, universal layer number but a property-specific transition that can be precisely identified by applying a multi-property scaling analysis, revealing distinct critical layer numbers for thermal, electrical, and optical behaviors.
proposals.method: We will fabricate high-quality, contamination-free FLG samples (1 to ~15 layers) via mechanical exfoliation and layer-by-layer assembly, confirmed by Raman spectroscopy and AFM. We will perform concurrent measurements of in-plane thermal conductivity (via micro-Raman thermometry or optothermal techniques), electrical conductivity (via van der Pauw geometry), and layer-dependent optical spectra (UV-Vis and FTIR). The data for each property will be analyzed using scaling laws of the form Property ∝ N^α, where N is layer count, to identify breaks in scaling exponents (α) that signify the crossover from 2D to quasi-2D to 3D regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, experimentally grounded map of the 2D-to-3D crossover in a van der Waals material across multiple fundamental physical properties. It will establish a generalized scaling analysis framework, adapted from polymer physics, for quantifying dimensional transitions in layered materials, directly informing the rational design of FLG-based devices with tailored thermal and electronic characteristics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of the multi-property scaling concept from polymer dynamics to the solid-state physics of 2D materials and the concurrent, correlated measurement of thermal, electrical, and optical properties across a wide layer range (1-15L) to deconvolve property-specific crossover points.

proposals.title: Mapping the 2D-to-3D Phononic Crossover: Thermal Conductivity of Few-Layer Graphene from 5 to 20 Layers
proposals.motivation: The thermal conductivity of few-layer graphene (FLG) exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on layer number, decreasing from single-layer to ~4 layers before increasing again, marking the onset of the 2D-to-3D crossover. This behavior is critical for understanding phonon transport dimensionality and for designing graphene-based thermal management materials. However, systematic experimental data for FLG with more than four layers is scarce, leaving the full crossover to bulk graphite poorly mapped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thermal conductivity of FLG will increase monotonically with layer number beyond 4 layers, asymptotically approaching the bulk graphite limit, as phonon modes transition from purely two-dimensional to three-dimensional and interlayer coupling modifies the phonon density of states and scattering rates.
proposals.method: We will fabricate high-quality, suspended FLG flakes with precisely controlled layer counts (5 to 20 layers) via mechanical exfoliation and layer-by-layer assembly, confirmed by Raman spectroscopy and optical contrast. Thermal conductivity will be measured using a non-contact, laser-based micro-Raman thermometry technique, where the temperature rise of the suspended flake under a focused laser is correlated with the shift of the G-peak. This method, previously established in the PI's lab for thinner flakes, will be extended with careful calibration for thicker samples to account for laser absorption and heat sinking effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive experimental dataset of thermal conductivity across the full 2D-to-3D crossover in FLG. It will validate and refine existing theoretical models for thickness-dependent phonon transport. The results will directly inform the design of graphene-based composites and electronic thermal interfaces where layer number is a key tuning parameter.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have focused on 1-4 layer graphene, this systematic investigation of 5+ layer FLG directly targets the poorly characterized regime where phonon dispersion and scattering transition from 2D to 3D behavior. The extension of precise micro-Raman thermometry to this thicker, yet still non-bulk, domain is a significant technical challenge.

proposals.title: Depth-Resolved Thermal Profiling of van der Waals Heterostructures via Seismic-Analogy Time-Domain Thermoreflectance
proposals.motivation: The cross-plane thermal conductivity of few-layer graphene (FLG) and its interfaces critically governs heat dissipation in next-generation nanoelectronics and quantum composites, yet its measurement remains a challenge. Conventional time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR) often yields only an effective thermal resistance, conflating the intrinsic conductivity of the layers with the interfacial thermal resistances (ITRs), which obscures the fundamental phonon transport mechanisms across van der Waals gaps.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal decay of the TDTR signal from a FLG stack contains distinct signatures of thermal wave reflections at internal interfaces, analogous to seismic reflections in subsurface geology, enabling the simultaneous and independent extraction of layer-specific cross-plane thermal conductivity and ITRs.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core principle of seismic reflection profiling by developing a multilayer thermal model based on the thermal wave equation, treating the ultrafast pump laser as a point source and the probe signal as a record of reflected thermal waves. The model will explicitly account for the reflection and transmission coefficients of thermal energy at each graphene/graphene interface. We will employ a Bayesian inversion algorithm, using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, to fit the simulated temporal TDTR response to experimental data, thereby extracting the depth-dependent thermal conductivity profile and ITR values as free parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new metrology for depth-resolved thermal property mapping in 2D material stacks, directly separating intrinsic conductivity from interfacial effects. It will provide unprecedented insight into cross-plane phonon transport and scattering in FLG, informing the thermal design of devices based on van der Waals heterostructures and quantum composites with tailored thermal properties.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and analytical adaptation of seismic reflection theory—a geophysical signal processing technique—to the inverse problem of nanoscale thermal transport, moving beyond conventional lumped-parameter TDTR analysis to achieve depth resolution within a stack of atomically thin layers.

proposals.title: Cross-Plane Thermal Transport in Few-Layer Graphene Stacks: A Time-Domain Thermoreflectance Study of Phonon Scattering and Quantum Confinement Effects
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of next-generation electronic and quantum devices based on 2D materials critically depends on understanding heat dissipation across van der Waals-bonded interfaces. Pre-2026, the cross-plane thermal conductivity of few-layer graphene (FLG) stacks remains poorly characterized, with significant discrepancies between theoretical predictions of phonon confinement and limited experimental data. This knowledge gap hinders the design of efficient thermal interfaces and quantum composites for high-power electronics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the cross-plane thermal conductivity of FLG stacks, measured via TDTR, will exhibit a non-monotonic dependence on layer number due to the competing effects of acoustic phonon confinement, which reduces the phonon group velocity, and interlayer coupling, which opens additional heat conduction channels.
proposals.method: We will fabricate FLG stacks (1 to ~10 layers) via mechanical exfoliation and deterministic transfer onto sapphire substrates with an optically opaque Al transducer layer. Cross-plane thermal conductivity will be measured using a custom time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR) system with a dual-frequency, phase-locked detection scheme. The data will be analyzed using a multilayer thermal model and a frequency-domain solution to the heat diffusion equation, fitting the experimental phase lag to extract the interface conductance and the FLG stack's intrinsic cross-plane thermal conductivity with high accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, direct measurement of cross-plane thermal conductivity in FLG stacks as a function of layer number, quantifying the role of phonon confinement and interlayer coupling. It will establish critical structure-property relationships for thermal management in 2D-material-based heterostructures and validate theoretical models of phonon transport in confined systems.
proposals.novelty: While TDTR is established for bulk and thin-film materials, its rigorous application to measure the intrinsic cross-plane conductivity of freestanding, atomically thin FLG stacks—separating interface effects from bulk-like behavior—is novel. This directly bridges Dr. Balandin's expertise in phonon physics and low-noise measurements with advanced thermal metrology.

proposals.title: Dimensional Crossover and Phonon Transport in Monolayer Hexagonal Boron Nitride: A Noise Spectroscopy and Raman Thermometry Study
proposals.motivation: Understanding thermal transport in two-dimensional (2D) materials is critical for managing heat in next-generation nanoelectronics. Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) is a key isostructural analog to graphene with a wide bandgap, offering a unique platform to study phonon-dominated thermal transport without electronic contributions. However, the fundamental limits of its thermal conductivity, the role of dimensionality, and the crossover from 2D to quasi-2D behavior with layer addition remain poorly quantified experimentally, especially for suspended monolayers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thermal conductivity of monolayer h-BN will exhibit a distinct dimensional crossover governed by the suppression of out-of-plane acoustic phonon modes and enhanced boundary scattering, leading to a non-monotonic layer-dependence that differs fundamentally from its electrical insulator properties.
proposals.method: We will fabricate suspended monolayer and few-layer h-BN membranes using mechanical exfoliation and transfer. Thermal conductivity will be measured via micro-Raman thermometry, where a focused laser locally heats the sample and the temperature rise is calibrated against the Raman peak shift. Concurrently, we will employ low-frequency electronic noise (1/f and Johnson noise) spectroscopy in fabricated metal-contacted devices to probe charge fluctuations linked to thermally activated defects and phonon interactions. Temperature-dependent measurements (80-500 K) will be performed to separate diffusive and ballistic phonon transport regimes. Data will be compared with first-principles Boltzmann transport equation (BTE) simulations of phonon spectra and scattering rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental quantification of thermal conductivity in suspended monolayer h-BN, establishing a benchmark for 2D insulators. It will elucidate the layer-dependent phonon scattering mechanisms and the dimensional crossover in thermal transport, offering critical insights for integrating h-BN as a heat-spreading or gate-dielectric material in 2D heterostructures. The correlation of thermal and noise metrics will advance diagnostic tools for material quality.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied h-BN on substrates, the thermal transport in *suspended* monolayers remains unexplored. The combined use of Raman thermometry and electronic noise spectroscopy to deconvolute phonon and defect-limited transport in an electrically insulating 2D system is a novel, multi-modal approach leveraging the PI's expertise.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Few-Layer Graphene Heterostructures for Ultralow Thermal Conductivity via Phonon Engineering
proposals.motivation: Enhancing thermoelectric efficiency requires materials with high electrical conductivity but low thermal conductivity. Few-layer graphene (FLG) offers excellent electronic transport, but its in-plane thermal conductivity is inherently high. An inverse design approach, targeting specific phonon scattering mechanisms, is needed to systematically suppress thermal transport in FLG without degrading its electronic properties, a key challenge in pre-2026 thermoelectrics research.
proposals.hypothesis: By computationally optimizing the stacking order, interlayer twist angles, and selective edge functionalization of FLG heterostructures, it is possible to create phononic band structures and scattering landscapes that minimize lattice thermal conductivity while preserving percolative electronic transport pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-scale computational framework. First, density functional theory (DFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) will calculate force constants and phonon spectra for candidate FLG configurations. A genetic algorithm will then solve the inverse problem, iteratively modifying structural variables (twist angle, layer number, defect patterning) to minimize the predicted thermal conductivity from the Boltzmann Transport Equation (BTE). Promising designs will be synthesized via CVD and layer transfer, with thermal conductivity measured using micro-Raman thermometry and electronic transport characterized via Hall effect and low-frequency noise spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct inverse-design methodology for tuning thermal transport in 2D materials, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will produce a new class of engineered FLG-based quantum composites with record-low thermal conductivity for their electrical mobility, providing a material platform for high-ZT, all-carbon thermoelectric devices.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying an inverse-design optimization loop specifically to phonon engineering in FLG heterostructures, integrating twistronics and defect engineering with the explicit goal of thermal conductivity minimization, rather than electronic band structure modification.

proposals.title: Probing Doping-Induced Electronic Structure Changes and Low-Frequency Noise in Metallic/Insulating Quantum Composite Multilayers
proposals.motivation: Quantum composites with charge-density-wave (CDW) fillers and wide-bandgap oxide heterostructures are promising for next-generation electronics and quantum materials. Pre-2026 work often treats metallic and insulating layers in multilayers as having similar electronic transport responses to doping. This assumption overlooks fundamental differences in how doping alters the electronic structure, charge density, and scattering mechanisms in adjacent layers, which critically govern device performance and noise characteristics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled doping will induce distinct, layer-specific electronic structure modifications in metallic and insulating components of a quantum composite multilayer, leading to a non-uniform redistribution of charge density and a measurable, anomalous increase in low-frequency current noise due to enhanced carrier scattering and modified phonon spectra at the interfaces.
proposals.method: We will fabricate tailored multilayers using pulsed laser deposition, combining a metallic CDW filler (e.g., 1T-TaS2) with an insulating wide-bandgap oxide (e.g., β-Ga2O3). Controlled doping will be achieved via in-situ stoichiometric variation (Al in Ga2O3) and electrostatic gating. Layer-specific electronic structure changes will be probed using temperature-dependent Raman spectroscopy (for phonons and CDW phase) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The critical link to electronic transport will be established through simultaneous measurements of current-voltage characteristics and low-frequency 1/f noise spectroscopy (1 Hz – 100 kHz) as a function of doping, temperature (10K – 500K), and charge density.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct experimental correlation between doping-modulated electronic structure, interfacial phonon dynamics, and the magnitude of the 1/f noise in quantum composite heterostructures. It will provide new design rules for low-noise electronic and quantum materials by identifying doping as a key parameter for controlling charge-density distribution and noise at metallic/insulating interfaces.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the standard assumption of uniform doping response and directly probing the disparate electronic structure changes in adjacent layers to explain macroscopic noise phenomena, a approach not previously applied to CDW/wide-bandgap oxide quantum composites.

proposals.title: Probing Rigid-Layer Compression Modes and Low-Frequency Noise Correlations in Ultrathin Films via Brillouin Light Scattering
proposals.motivation: The low-frequency 1/f noise in electronic devices, such as Schottky diodes, is a critical reliability metric often linked to material quality and phonon dynamics. Pre-2026 work, including Balandin's studies on β-(AlxGa1−x)2O3 diodes and phonons in boron nitride films, suggests that rigid-layer shear and compression modes in van der Waals and ultra-thin films can influence charge transport and noise. Brillouin light scattering (BLS) is a powerful, non-contact technique to probe acoustic phonons in the GHz range, but its systematic application to correlate these specific low-frequency vibrational modes with electronic noise in functional materials remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spectral density and damping of rigid-layer compression modes in ultrathin films, directly measurable via BLS, are fundamentally linked to the magnitude and character of low-frequency electronic 1/f noise in devices fabricated from these materials.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-contrast, multi-pass tandem Fabry-Perot interferometer-based Brillouin spectrometer to probe acoustic phonon modes in the 1-100 GHz range. Ultrathin films of materials like hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) and transition metal dichalcogenides (e.g., MoS2), relevant to Balandin's work on quantum composites and wide-bandgap electronics, will be synthesized or exfoliated onto diamond substrates to enhance phonon propagation and BLS signal. Concurrently, we will fabricate simple two-terminal or Schottky diode structures on the same films to perform standardized low-frequency noise spectroscopy measurements, enabling direct correlation between the BLS-derived phonon properties and the measured electronic noise power spectral density.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel experimental framework linking specific, measurable vibrational properties to electronic noise, moving beyond empirical correlations. It will provide fundamental insight into how layer-layer interactions in thin films govern noise generation, directly impacting the design of low-noise electronic and optoelectronic devices. The findings will advance the understanding of phonon-engineered materials for quantum composites and next-generation diodes.
proposals.novelty: While BLS is a known phonon probe and noise is a standard electronic measurement, their combined, correlated application to identify the specific role of rigid-layer modes in electronic noise generation is novel. This approach integrates Balandin's expertise in noise, phonons, and novel materials into a unique diagnostic methodology.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Reflection Profiling for Cross-Plane Thermal Conductivity Measurement in FLG Stacks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principle of seismic reflection profiling from geophysics to measure cross-plane thermal transport in few-layer graphene (FLG) stacks using time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR). In seismology, the structure and properties of subsurface layers are determined by analyzing the time delay and amplitude of reflected seismic waves. Analogously, in this adapted method, the ultrafast thermal pulse from the TDTR pump laser acts as the source, and the thermoreflectance signal is treated as a record of 'thermal reflections' from the interfaces within the FLG stack. By developing a multilayer thermal model that accounts for the reflection and transmission of thermal waves at each van der Waals interface, the temporal evolution of the TDTR signal can be inversely analyzed to simultaneously extract the cross-plane thermal conductivity of the FLG layers and the interfacial thermal resistances, providing a depth-resolved thermal profile of the stack.

ideas.title: Quantifying and decoupling the substrate interaction in suspended FLG via on-flake supported reference
ideas.description: This study directly confronts the assumption of negligible substrate effects by fabricating a dual-measurement platform on a single, continuous few-layer graphene flake. One portion is fully suspended, while an adjacent portion is supported on an atomically flat, low-disorder hexagonal boron nitride substrate, with both regions sharing identical contact electrodes. By performing simultaneous layer-dependent measurements of carrier mobility, thermal conductivity, and Raman spectra on both the suspended and supported segments, the experiment explicitly quantifies the substrate's influence on each property as a function of layer number. This approach provides a definitive, internally controlled map of intrinsic versus substrate-modified properties in FLG.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Decoupling of Substrate Effects on FLG Thermal Transport: Scattering, Strain, and Doping
ideas.description: This study moves beyond correlation to establish causation by systematically isolating the physical mechanisms through which substrates alter FLG thermal conductivity. Using suspended FLG devices as a reference, we sequentially and controllably introduce substrate coupling, applied strain, and electrostatic doping while measuring thermal conductivity. The experiment is coupled with simultaneous spectroscopic and scanning probe characterization to quantify the individual contributions of interfacial phonon scattering, strain-induced phonon band shifts, and carrier-phonon interactions. The integrated approach yields a predictive, mechanistic model for substrate engineering of 2D material thermal properties.

ideas.title: Thermal Impedance Spectroscopy for Quantum Dot Ensemble Characterization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) to thermal transport. Instead of an AC voltage, a small oscillatory temperature gradient is applied across a quantum dot ensemble film, and the complex thermal impedance (amplitude and phase shift of the heat wave) is measured across a spectrum of frequencies. Analogous to how an EIS Nyquist plot deconvolutes charge transfer, capacitance, and diffusion, the thermal impedance spectrum will have features sensitive to different scattering length scales. High-frequency components are scattered by small objects like QD cores, making them probe the size distribution. Mid-frequency components interact with interfaces, probing disorder and ligand shell properties. Low-frequency components are affected by larger-scale film morphology. By fitting the spectrum to a multi-scale thermal resistor-capacitor network model, one can extract quantitative, separate metrics for QD size dispersion and interface quality from a single, non-destructive measurement, providing a sensitive dual probe of nanostructure.

ideas.title: Isolating Intrinsic Properties by Engineering a Suspended, Contact-Controlled Platform
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that substrate effects are negligible in electronic measurements of few-layer graphene (FLG) by drawing an analogy from protein biochemistry. In that field, the intrinsic function of a protein is isolated from its complex cellular environment using in vitro reconstitution—purifying the protein and measuring its activity in a controlled buffer with a standardized assay. Adapted to condensed matter physics, the research involves fabricating a platform where FLG samples of varying layer counts are all suspended over identical trenches, with metallic contacts patterned in an identical geometry on each flake. This engineered system acts as the 'controlled buffer' (removing the variable dielectric influence of a substrate) and 'standardized assay' (controlling for contact resistance and geometry), allowing for the direct measurement and comparison of intrinsic layer-dependent electronic properties to definitively test the substrate assumption.

ideas.title: Template-Directed Assembly of 3D Quantum Dot Arrays for Engineered Thermal Transport
ideas.description: This project designs and fabricates three-dimensional quantum dot (QD) arrays with precisely controlled geometry using a hierarchical, template-directed approach. A pre-defined, porous 3D scaffold establishes the target lattice structure and inter-dot spacing, into which QDs are uniformly incorporated via controlled infiltration and binding processes. This method decouples the challenge of 3D array geometry from the QD synthesis, enabling systematic characterization of how array topology (e.g., simple cubic vs. diamond lattice) influences phonon transport and thermal conductivity. The goal is to establish design rules for 3D QD-based metamaterials with tunable, anisotropic, or ultralow thermal conductivity for advanced thermal management.

ideas.title: Adapt Cryogenic Thermal Conductivity Measurement Using Astrophysical Dust Detection Techniques
ideas.description: The original idea is to measure thermal conductivity at cryogenic temperatures below 10K to test model limits. The analogous problem from astrophysics is detecting faint infrared emission from cold interstellar dust (below 10K) against a noisy background. That field solved it by using detectors cooled to sub-Kelvin temperatures to minimize intrinsic noise and employing modulated signal (chopping) with lock-in amplification to isolate the weak signal. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to design a thermal conductivity measurement platform for the sub-10K regime that integrates a dilution refrigerator (to reach a base temperature below 100mK for the detector environment), a modulated, microfabricated on-sample heater, and a transition-edge sensor (TES) as the ultra-sensitive thermometer. By applying a known, oscillatory heat flux and using lock-in detection on the TES output, the experiment can precisely measure the resulting temperature wave's amplitude and phase, thereby extracting thermal conductivity with unprecedented signal-to-noise at these very low temperatures, directly testing the predictions of phonon and electron transport models in the boundary scattering and quantum regime.

ideas.title: Extend crossover study to more than four layers using a multi-property scaling analysis
ideas.description: The original idea to measure thermal conductivity for FLG with 5+ layers to map the 2D-to-3D crossover is analogous to studying the unentangled-to-entangled crossover in polymer melts. In polymer physics, this is solved by measuring multiple dynamic properties across a wide chain length range and using scaling analysis to pinpoint the transition. Adapting this solution, the research should be expanded to measure a suite of properties—thermal conductivity, electrical conductivity, and layer-dependent optical spectra—for FLG samples from 1 to approximately 15 layers. A concurrent scaling analysis of all properties against layer number will reveal the precise, property-dependent layer counts at which 2D behavior transitions to 3D behavior, providing a complete, multi-faceted map of the dimensional crossover.

ideas.title: Adapting Temperature-Dependent Contact Resistance Models from Microelectronics to Superconductor Interfaces
ideas.description: This idea challenges the common assumption of a temperature-independent interface transparency parameter in superconductor heterostructures by drawing an analogy from semiconductor microelectronics. In that field, the temperature dependence of metal-semiconductor contact resistance is well-established and characterized using physical models (thermionic emission/tunneling) and dedicated test structures like Transmission Line Models (TLM). The analogous solution is adapted by proposing the design of superconducting test junctions to isolate interface effects, followed by systematic measurement of current-voltage characteristics across a temperature range from below Tc to above. The data is then fitted to a modified theoretical framework (e.g., extending the BTK model) where the transparency parameter is explicitly treated as a function of temperature, potentially revealing its dependence on factors like thermal smearing of quasiparticle states, temperature-dependent gap profiles, and phonon-assisted tunneling processes.

ideas.title: Disentangle and model competing physical mechanisms for temperature-dependent superconducting interface transparency
ideas.description: This research program aims to move beyond a simple phenomenological measurement of temperature-dependent interface transparency by identifying and quantifying the specific, competing physical mechanisms responsible. It combines a coordinated experimental suite capable of probing structural, electronic, and superconducting properties of the same interface as a function of temperature with a computational model that incorporates mechanisms like thermal strain, interdiffusion, and barrier evolution. The core advance is the adversarial validation of the model against the multi-faceted dataset, allowing the dominant mechanism in a given material system to be identified. This enables predictive design of interfaces with optimized, stable transparency across the operational temperature range.

ideas.title: Temperature-dependent thermal transport in FLG: An analogical approach from geophysical seismic tomography
ideas.description: This idea expands the original temperature-dependent study of FLG thermal transport by drawing an analogy from geophysical seismic tomography. In seismology, the temperature-dependent propagation speed of seismic waves is used to model the Earth's internal thermal structure. Adapting this, the research will treat the temperature-dependent thermal conductivity of FLG as a probe for its internal phonon scattering landscape. Instead of merely recording conductivity vs. temperature, the data will be analyzed using models to deconvolve the individual contributions of different scattering mechanisms (boundary, defect, Umklapp) as a function of temperature and layer number. This approach transforms a standard transport measurement into a diagnostic tool for mapping the dominant thermal resistance processes across different temperature regimes in 2D layered materials.

ideas.description: This project aims to discover general structural principles—specific stacking sequences or twist angles—that enforce high and increasing thermal conductivity with layer number, rather than finding isolated examples. It employs a closed-loop, high-throughput computational pipeline that screens layered material configurations using classical and quantum mechanical simulations. The key innovation is using machine learning to guide the search towards configurations where the inverted trend is robust to realistic disorder and strain, as predicted by a learned descriptor for phonon band stability. The outcome will be a set of design rules for stacking-engineered thermal management materials, validated by targeted experimental synthesis on promising candidates.

These independent scattering parameters are then mapped onto the performance of fabricated nanotransport devices with controlled dot size and spacing. The result is a definitive, quantitative ranking of scattering source limitations, moving beyond a simple binary test to establish a scaling law for conduction in dot-based materials.

ideas.title: 3D Quantum Dot Phononic Crystal via Colloidal Self-Assembly
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from photonic crystal fabrication—colloidal self-assembly of sacrificial templates—to the problem of scaling up to 3D isotropic quantum dot arrays for thermal management. In optics, 3D photonic crystals are created by self-assembling monodisperse colloidal spheres into close-packed lattices, which are then infiltrated and inverted to produce periodic dielectric structures that control light. By applying this method, we propose to fabricate a 3D 'phononic' crystal of quantum dots: a sacrificial opal template of polymer spheres will be assembled, infiltrated with quantum dot material (e.g., PbS, CdSe, or perovskite nanocrystals), and subsequently removed. This will yield a scalable, isotropic 3D array where the periodic arrangement of quantum dots and air voids is designed to manipulate phonon transport, potentially enhancing thermal insulation or directing heat flow in semiconductor devices.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Anisotropy Tomography to Map Thermal Anisotropy in QD Superlattices
ideas.description: This idea adapts the geophysical solution for mapping seismic anisotropy in the Earth's mantle to the problem of measuring thermal anisotropy in quantum dot (QD) superlattices. In seismology, directional differences in shear-wave velocity are tomographically reconstructed to reveal deformation fabrics. The adapted approach applies an analogous 'thermal tomography' concept: using a localized, directional thermal probe (e.g., a focused laser for thermoreflectance or a microfabricated 3ω sensor) to apply and detect thermal transients at multiple in-plane and out-of-plane angles relative to the superlattice layers. By analyzing the directional dependence of thermal decay constants, one can reconstruct the full thermal conductivity tensor and map spatial variations in anisotropy, providing insight into how QD ordering, spacing, and interfacial quality influence directional heat flow at the nanoscale.

ideas.title: Adapt Phonon-Hopping Model Across Material Systems Using Alloy Mapping Framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational alloy design, where models are extended across material systems via descriptor mapping. The analogous problem—applying a property prediction model from one validated alloy system to a new one—is solved by identifying fundamental, system-agnostic dimensionless descriptors that govern the target property and using them to re-parameterize the model's core equations. Adapted to the QD superlattice context, the research involves: 1) Identifying universal dimensionless descriptors for phonon-mediated hopping (e.g., normalized phonon frequency ratios, dielectric contrasts, and scaled coupling strengths) from the original validated model; 2) Developing a mapping function that expresses the model's inputs using these descriptors calculated from basic material properties of any new QD system; 3) Applying this adapted framework to predict phonon-hopping behavior in disparate material systems such as III-V (InAs/GaAs), IV-VI (PbSe), and halide perovskite QD superlattices, thereby systematically testing and extending the model's generality beyond its original validation scope.

ideas.title: Adapting seismic interferometry to map phonon wavefields in QD superlattices via coherent X-ray scattering
ideas.description: This idea adapts the geophysical technique of seismic interferometry to the nanoscale study of phonons. In seismology, the cross-correlation of ambient seismic noise recorded at different stations is used to extract the impulse response (Green's function) between them, enabling subsurface imaging without controlled sources. Here, we apply this concept to probe phonons in quantum dot (QD) superlattices using coherent X-ray scattering. The inherent, stochastic thermal excitation of phonons within the superlattice generates a constantly fluctuating, diffuse X-ray scattering pattern when illuminated by a coherent X-ray beam (e.g., from a free-electron laser). By performing intensity-intensity cross-correlation analysis (via X-ray photon correlation spectroscopy, XPCS) on this diffuse speckle, measured from different spatial regions or at different time delays, we can mathematically reconstruct the phonon dynamical response. This allows for the direct, real-space imaging of phonon wavefront propagation, the mapping of scattering events at QD interfaces and defects, and the visualization of Anderson localization pathways for specific phonon modes within the disordered potential of the superlattice, providing unprecedented insight into thermal transport and energy dissipation at the nanoscale.

ideas.title: Optically Excited, Heterodyne-Enhanced Coherent X-ray Scattering for Mode-Selective Phonon Dynamics in Disordered Nanostructures
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach uses an optical pump to impulsively excite specific phonon modes within a quantum dot superlattice, creating a coherent, time-varying lattice displacement. A phase-stable, high-repetition-rate XFEL probe beam, split into a signal and a reference path, then performs heterodyne coherent X-ray scattering. The scattered signal is mixed with the reference beam, amplifying the weak, phonon-induced modulations in the speckle pattern. By recording a stroboscopic movie of this amplified scattering across time delays, the system directly resolves the amplitude, phase, dispersion, and spatial localization of the selected phonon modes, effectively filtering out the dominant static disorder through differential time-domain analysis.

ideas.title: Adapting Coarse-Grained Protein-Ligand Docking to Map Phonon Scattering at Disordered QD Interfaces
ideas.description: This idea adapts a multi-scale computational strategy from computational biophysics to the problem of modeling phonon scattering at disordered QD interfaces. In drug discovery, the analogous problem of sampling the vast conformational space of protein-ligand binding is solved by first using fast, coarse-grained docking algorithms to identify promising states, followed by detailed all-atom MD on only those states. We adapt this by first employing a coarse-grained lattice dynamics or Monte Carlo approach to stochastically generate and pre-screen thousands of atomistic models of the disordered interface region, ranking them by a simple scattering metric. Subsequently, a strategically chosen subset of these interface models—spanning the range of disorder (e.g., different roughness amplitudes, defect densities, or alloy mixing profiles)—becomes the input for computationally intensive, full-scale molecular dynamics simulations. This two-step protocol makes the systematic exploration of the disorder parameter space tractable, enabling the extraction of statistically meaningful phonon scattering properties directly linked to specific interface characteristics.
